content:
Fan Chen — Researcher Analysis Report Analysis Mode: Fast | Analysis Time: 2026-03-16T00:41:44
Rating: Active Researcher (41.4/100) Basic Metrics Metric Value Institution Unknown h-index 12 Total Citations 542 Citations in Last 5 Years 287 Number of Papers 33 Papers Published in Top Journals 0 Publication Period 1992 - 2022 Semantic Scholar 49040579 Research Trajectory The “Fan Chen” in this dataset is likely a combined record of multiple researchers with the same name, not a complete trajectory of a single scholar. From the content of his papers, at least two distinct types of researchers can be identified: (1) A researcher who has been engaged in analytical chemistry/food chemistry research since 1992, with his work evolving from forensic gas chromatography in the early days to ion liquid extraction and natural deep eutectic solvents (NADES); (2) A group of computer science papers published after 2019, focusing on topics such as reinforcement learning, ReRAM accelerators, quantum computing, optimization theory, diffusion models, etc. These papers have nothing to do with the previous research and clearly belong to different groups of researchers.
In terms of the main area of analytical chemistry, his academic trajectory is clear: from 1992 to 2008, he focused on forensic toxicological gas chromatography detection. In the 2010s, he turned to HPLC-DAD food/drug fingerprinting. In 2015-2016, he made a significant breakthrough by introducing ion liquids into liquid microextraction and countercurrent chromatography, which was published in Food Chemistry and J. Chromatography A, with 85 and 61 citations respectively. From 2019 to 2021, he explored the field of green extraction using natural deep eutectic solvents (NADES), publishing several high-citation papers and establishing himself as an academic leader in this niche area of green analytical chemistry.
His h-index of 12, total citations of 542, and the ratio of 287 citations in the last 5 years indicate that he is a mid-level researcher whose influence continues to grow in a specific niche area, such as ion liquids/NADES for green pretreatment, rather than a cross-disciplinary expert.
Breakthrough Works 1. Determination of chlorophenols in honey samples using in-situ ionic liquid-dispersive liquid-liquid microextraction as a pretreatment method followed by high-performance liquid chromatography (2015) Description: Proposed an in-situ ionic liquid dispersion liquid-liquid microextraction method. By using hydrophilic ionic liquid [C4MIM][BF4] to react with anion exchange to generate hydrophobic extractant [C4MIM][NTf2], it was possible to enrich six chlorophenols in honey and detect them using HPLC. This method was cited 85 times.
Why it couldn’t be done before: Traditional DLLME relied on toxic organic solvents like chlorobenzene and carbon tetrachloride as extractants. There were many interfering substances in food matrices, and the strategy of generating ionic liquids in situ had not been systematically applied to the analysis of trace pollutants in honey before. Additionally, the cost of commercial ionic liquids decreased significantly in the late 2010s, making this method feasible.
Impact: It opened up a practical path for ionic liquid microextraction in the field of food safety. Many subsequent papers followed this approach, extending it to complex matrices like wine and vegetable oils, becoming a landmark paper in this niche area.
2. Determination of Alternaria mycotoxins in wine and juice using ionic liquid modified countercurrent chromatography as a pretreatment method followed by high-performance liquid chromatography (2016) Description: Introduced ionic liquids into the countercurrent chromatography solvent system to achieve preparatory enrichment and purification of Alternaria mycotoxins in wine and juice. This work was published in J. Chromatography A and cited 61 times.
Why it couldn’t be done before: Countercurrent chromatography traditionally relied on standard solvent systems like n-hexane/ethyl acetate/methanol/water. Its selectivity for polar mycotoxins was limited. The adjustable polarity of ionic liquids had not been systematically integrated into countercurrent chromatography systems before 2015, and the low purity of ionic liquids in the early days led to unstable phase separation.
Impact: It demonstrated the feasibility of ionic liquid-modified countercurrent chromatography as a pretreatment tool for natural products and food contaminants. This led to a series of related works involving oil separation and anthocyanin purification.
3. Selective microextraction of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons using a hydrophobic deep eutectic solvent composed with an iron oxide-based nanoferrofluid (2019) Description: Combined hydrophobic deep eutectic solvents with iron oxide nanofluids to achieve magnetic-assisted selective microextraction of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons. This work was published in Microchimica Acta and cited 40 times.
Why it couldn’t be done before: Hydrophobic NADES wasn’t systematically synthesized and characterized until around 2017. Combining DES with magnetic nanoparticles to create “magnetic DLLME” required both DES design and nanofluid preparation techniques, which were rarely explored before 2019.
Impact: It promoted the application of DES in environmental pollutant detection. This solution, which combines green solvents and magnetic separation, was widely used in subsequent research.
4. A priori design of new natural deep eutectic solvent for lutein recovery from microalgae (2021) Description: Based on a prior design framework for intermolecular hydrogen bonding interactions, this study systematically screened NADES for green extraction of microalgae lutein. This work was published in Food Chemistry and cited 74 times.
Why it couldn’t be done before: Previous NADES research relied heavily on trial-and-error experiments, lacking rational design methods based on molecular structure prediction of solubility. The combination of molecular dynamics simulations and Hansen solubility parameters in food extraction NADES design didn’t become mature until the early 2020s.
Impact: It shifted NADES research from empirical synthesis to prior design driven by mechanisms, providing a methodological example for green extraction chemistry. This had a direct impact on the direction of microalgae biorefinery.
5. A Theoretical Study on Terpene‐Based Natural Deep Eutectic Solvent: Relationship between Viscosity and Hydrogen‐Bonding Interactions (2021) Description: Through quantum chemistry calculations and molecular dynamics simulations, this study explained the microscopic mechanism behind the low viscosity of terpene-based NADES. The relationship between viscosity and hydrogen bonding interactions was quantified. This work was published in Global Challenges and cited 72 times.
Why it couldn’t be done before: High viscosity was a major obstacle to the industrialization of NADES, but its microscopic causes were unclear for a long time. Terpene-based low viscosity systems hadn’t been studied systematically before 2019. Systematically linking DFT calculations with rheological measurements required interdisciplinary integration between computational chemistry and analytical chemistry.
Impact: It theoretically explained the viscosity advantages of terpene-based NADES, providing a thermodynamic/dynamic basis for the directed design of low-viscosity green solvents. This promoted the development of such solvents for practical separation applications.
Research Directions Ion liquid-assisted microextraction and countercurrent chromatography (for analyzing trace pollutants in food and environment) Design, characterization, and green extraction applications of natural deep eutectic solvents Development of chromatographic detection methods for forensic and food analysis (GC, HPLC, LC-MS) Preparative separation and purification of bioactive natural products Methodological Evolution The methodological evolution of this analytical chemist shows a clear three-stage transition. In the first stage (1992–2008), gas chromatography (GC/FPD, GC-MS) was the core tool, focusing on quantitative detection of forensic toxins and food components. The methods were limited to single components and matrices, with limited technical depth. In the second stage (2010–2014), multi-component simultaneous determination using HPLC-DAD/ELSD became common, and attempts were made to build traditional Chinese medicine fingerprinting systems. However, technological innovation was still limited.
The third stage (since 2015) represents a fundamental paradigm shift: researchers introduced ion liquids and deep eutectic solvents into sample pretreatment processes. The core innovation shifted from “detection methods themselves” to “green and efficient sample enrichment/separation strategies.” This shift aligns with the overall trend of “green analytical chemistry” in the field of analytical chemistry, upgrading the work from technology application to method innovation, resulting in a significant increase in citations. More recently (2021), quantum chemistry calculations and molecular dynamics simulations were introduced, elevating empirical solvent design to rationally designed methods. This marks a higher level of methodological evolution towards computationally assisted experiments.
Domain Impact In the sub-field of analytical chemistry (green sample pretreatment methods), this researcher is one of the key contributors to the coupling of ion liquids with countercurrent chromatography and the extraction of natural deep eutectic solvents. His representative papers (Food Chemistry 2015, J. Chromatography A 2016, 2021 series NADES papers) contributed approximately 470 citations, accounting for 87% of the total citations. This indicates that his influence is highly concentrated during the development of green pretreatment methods from 2015 to 2021. His work has promoted the application of NADES from concept verification to practical applications in food safety and natural product separation. However, he has not produced disruptive results that would change the cognitive framework of the entire analytical chemistry field. His influence is limited to the niche area of green analytical methods. It should be noted that the large number of computer science papers (reinforcement learning, quantum computing, optimization algorithms, etc.) appearing after 2019 are from other researchers with the same name and should not be attributed to this researcher.
Top 20 Cited Papers No. Year Citations Title 1 2015 85 Determination of chlorophenols in honey samples using in-situ ionic liquid-dispersive liquid-liquid microextraction as a pretreatment method followed by high-performance liquid chromatography. 2 2021 74 A priori design of new natural deep eutectic solvent for lutein recovery from microalgae. 3 2021 72 A Theoretical Study on Terpene‐Based Natural Deep Eutectic Solvent: Relationship between Viscosity and Hydrogen‐Bonding Interactions 4 2016 61 Determination of Alternaria mycotoxins in wine and juice using ionic liquid modified countercurrent chromatography as a pretreatment method followed by high-performance liquid chromatography. 5 2021 43 Terpenoid-capric acid based natural deep eutectic solvent: Insight into the nature of low viscosity 6 2019 40 Selective microextraction of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons using a hydrophobic deep eutectic solvent composed with an iron oxide-based nanoferrofluid 7 2021 40 New deep eutectic solvent based superparamagnetic nanofluid for determination of perfluoroalkyl substances in edible oils. 8 2021 18 New natural deep eutectic solvents based on aromatic organic acids 9 2022 16 Overview of Solvent System Selection Strategies for Countercurrent Chromatography 10 2021 15 Why do ammonium salt/phenol-based deep eutectic solvents show low viscosity? 11 2015 14 Determination of chlorophenols in red wine using ionic liquid countercurrent chromatography as a new pretreatment method followed by high-performance liquid chromatography. 12 2020 13 A biphasic system based on guanidinium ionic liquid: Preparative separation of eicosapentaenoic acid ethyl ester and docosahexaenoic acid ethyl ester by countercurrent chromatography. 13 2020 12 Preparative separation of high-purity trans- and cis-ferulic acid from wheat bran by pH-zone-refining counter-current chromatography. 14 2020 10 Ionic liquid-modified countercurrent chromatographic isolation of high-purity delphinidin-3-rutinoside from eggplant peel. 15 2021 7 Pretreatments of wheat straw for possibility use in maintenance-free compressed green roof substrates 16 1992 6 Determination of the concentration of tetramethylenedisulfotetramine in human blood by GC/FPD. 17 1993 4 Determination of tetramethylenedisulfotetramine in blood by gas chromatography. 18 2019 4 Efficient separation of tocopherol homologues in vegetable oil by ionic liquid based countercurrent chromatography using a non-aqueous biphasic system. 19 2003 3 ANALYSIS ON CONSTITUENTS OF LIQUEFIED PRODUCTSFROM WHEAT STRAW 20 2021 2 Allyl glycidyl ether-modified animal glue binder for improved water resistance and bonding strength in sand casting

content:
Hsin-Pai Cheng — Researcher Analysis Report Analysis Mode: fast | Analysis Time: 2026-03-16T00:37:41
Rating: Active Researcher (45.7/100) Basic Metrics Metric Value Institution Unknown h-index 13 Total Citations 450 Citations in Last 5 Years 7 Number of Papers Published 29 Papers Published in Top Journals 7 Publication Period 2016 - 2024 Semantic Scholar 3461946 Research Trajectory Hsin-Pai Cheng is a researcher specializing in efficient AI systems and edge deployment. His academic trajectory can be divided into three clear stages. From 2016 to 2018, he focused on the IBM TrueNorth neuromorphic computing platform, studying the quantization of neural networks under hardware constraints and their robustness against adversarial attacks. This stage laid the foundation for his dual perspective on algorithms and hardware.
From 2019 to 2020 was a period of high productivity and influence. He led the review article “Low-Power Computer Vision” (63 citations), which defined the subfield of low-power computer vision. In the same year, several NAS papers (MSNet, AutoShrink, SwiftNet) focused on architecture search for IoT/edge devices. The paper “Ordering Chaos” (MLSys, 58 citations) solved the engineering bottleneck in memory scheduling for irregular NAS networks, marking a key step forward in practical deployment of NAS research.
After 2021, his output slowed significantly (almost no publications in 2021). From 2022 to 2024, his work focused on zero-sample NAS and efficient Transformers. In 2025-2026, new papers emerged in the field of end-to-end autonomous driving systems (RoCA, FALO, generative scene generation). Given the lack of institutional information, it is speculated that he has entered the industrial sector, shifting his focus to edge deployment of autonomous driving systems. Overall, he is a “efficiency engineer” rather than a pioneer in a new field, but he has made significant contributions to low-power AI deployment.
Breakthrough Works 1. Low-Power Computer Vision: Status, Challenges, and Opportunities (2019) Description: This paper systematically reviewed the current state, challenges, and opportunities in low-power computer vision, covering model compression, hardware acceleration, and mobile device deployment. It became a landmark review article in this field.
Why it couldn’t be done before: Before 2018, the mobile AI ecosystem was not yet mature. Lightweight models like MobileNet/ShuffleNet were just starting to emerge, NAS methods were not widely used, and there was insufficient data for actual chip applications (such as Kirin, A-series NPUs), making it impossible to produce a systematic review based on real-world data. 2019 was the right time for industrial implementation and academic breakthroughs.
Impact: With 63 citations, this paper became his most cited work. It provided a unified framework and reference for low-power vision research, promoting systematic attention to “edge computing efficiency.”
2. Ordering Chaos: Memory-Aware Scheduling of Irregularly Wired Neural Networks for Edge Devices (2020) Description: This paper proposed a memory-aware scheduling algorithm for irregularly wired neural networks, enabling efficient operation on edge devices with limited memory. It filled the gap between NAS design and practical deployment.
Why it couldn’t be done before: Irregularly wired NAS networks like Random Wiring Network only emerged in 2018-2019, so the problem of memory scheduling was new. Previously, regular networks like VGG and ResNet had fixed computation graphs, making scheduling simple. Meanwhile, MLSys, a system-and-machine learning conference, was founded in 2019, providing a platform for publishing papers. The key insight was to treat irregular graphs found in NAS research as scheduling optimization problems rather than pure algorithm problems.
Impact: Published at MLSys 2020 (a top system conference), it received 58 citations. It clarified the paradigm that “NAS should not only search for accuracy but also deployability,” influencing subsequent research on hardware-aware NAS.
3. MAT: A Multi-strength Adversarial Training Method to Mitigate Adversarial Attacks (2018) Description: This paper proposed a multi-intensity adversarial training strategy, which significantly improved the robustness of DNNs against various types of adversarial attacks while reducing the loss of accuracy for clean samples.
Why it couldn’t be done before: The problem of adversarial examples was first proposed by Goodfellow et al. in 2014, but early defense methods (such as single-intensity training) quickly became ineffective against strong attacks. The emergence of PGD attacks and C&W attacks in 2017-2018 revealed the limitations of single-intensity training, leading to the need for multi-intensity strategies. In terms of computational resources, generating multiple rounds of adversarial examples and training was too costly before 2017.
Impact: With 29 citations, this paper was one of his most cited algorithmic works. It made significant contributions to methods for improving adversarial robustness and provided a foundation for subsequent research on neuromorphic computing security (AdverQuil, 2019).
4. NASGEM: Neural Architecture Search via Graph Embedding Method (2020) Description: This paper introduced graph embedding methods into NAS, using graph neural networks to encode the topological structure of architectures and constructing performance predictors. This resulted in a more flexible and scalable NAS framework based on estimators.
Why it couldn’t be done before: Predictor-based NAS was just starting to emerge before 2019, and the encoding methods were mostly simple sequences or manual features. Graph neural networks began to mature in 2018-2019 (such as GraphSAGE, GIN), but using GNNs for architectural topology encoding became feasible only in 2019-2020.
Impact: Published at AAAI 2020, it received 24 citations. It promoted the NAS technology of “graph-based architecture representation” and was one of his two peak outputs in 2020, along with Ordering Chaos.
5. Towards Decentralized Deep Learning with Differential Privacy (2019) Description: This paper proposed algorithms for protecting privacy in decentralized learning frameworks, maintaining good convergence speed and communication efficiency while respecting privacy budgets. It was aimed at federated learning scenarios.
Why it couldn’t be done before: The combination of differential privacy and deep learning (such as DP-SGD) was proposed by Abadi et al. in 2016, but it mainly targeted centralized scenarios at that time. Decentralized training without parameter servers had high communication costs and complex privacy analysis before 2018. With advancements in distributed optimization theory and communication compression technology, conditions were met to handle both dimensions simultaneously in 2019.
Impact: With 36 citations, this paper received significant attention in the field of privacy-protected distributed learning, demonstrating its research scope from hardware efficiency to system security.
Research Areas Efficient neural network design and edge deployment (quantization, NAS, memory scheduling) Low-power computer vision Adversarial attacks and defenses Neuromorphic computing (early stage) End-to-end autonomous driving systems (recent focus) Methodological Evolution Cheng’s methodological evolution shows a three-stage transformation from “single-point optimization” to “system-level coordination” and then to “task-driven”. In the early stages (2016-2018), empirical algorithms were used: compensation learning strategies were proposed for IBM TrueNorth’s quantization losses, and multi-intensity training data augmentation was designed for adversarial attacks. These methods were direct and verifiable, but their focus was limited to a single dimension.
From 2019 to 2020, his methodology evolved into a systematic approach of “searching + graph structure + constraint awareness”. NAS works generally introduced graph representations (NASGEM used GNNs to encode architectural topologies, MSNet/SwiftNet used graph propagation for meta-knowledge). The optimization goals expanded from single accuracy to multi-objectives such as accuracy × latency × memory. Ordering Chaos further transformed the problem into scheduling optimization, crossing the boundaries between algorithms and systems. After 2022, zero-sample NAS (ZiCo-BC) emerged, attempting to replace expensive architecture evaluations with training-independent metrics, representing a extreme example of efficiency pursuit. Papers on autonomous driving from 2025 onwards reflect an engineering transformation from “how to design efficient models” to “how to deploy in real-world closed-loop systems”.
Domain Impact Cheng has a clear but medium-scale influence in the field of low-power/edge AI: h-index 13, total citations 450. Two of his core papers (Low-Power CV review with 63 citations, Ordering Chaos with 58 citations) contributed about one-fourth of the citations. His contributions mainly lie in “breaking through the last mile between NAS and practical deployment” - this engineering problem is often overlooked in academia. The publication of Ordering Chaos at MLSys marked the formal recognition of this issue by the system community. Only 7 citations in the past five years indicate that his academic activity has significantly declined, suggesting that he has shifted his focus to industrial research. Overall, he is a valuable contributor to the field of edge AI deployment, but he has not reached the level of being a pioneer in a new research paradigm.

Ismail Bayram — Research Analyst Report Analysis Mode: fast | Analysis Time: 2026-03-16T00:58:48
Rating: Active Researcher (37.1/100) Basic Metrics Metric Value Institution Unknown h-index 11 Total Citations 479 Citations in Last 5 Years 31 Number of Papers Published 83 Number of Papers Presented at Top Conferences 0 Publication Duration 1999 - 2025 Semantic Scholar 144592206 Research Trajectory Ismail Bayram is a practical researcher in the field of agricultural/ veterinary science in Turkey. His research career spanned from 1999 to 2025, with his main focus being on the nutritional sciences of poultry (quails, broilers, laying hens) and ruminants (sheep, buffalo, dairy cows). His academic journey began with basic feed additives such as probiotics, vitamins, and fish meal alternatives. Between 2006 and 2013, he developed a research direction focused on natural herbal extracts like oregano, fennel, poppy seeds, and mint. In 2008, his paper using ultrasound technology to predict carcass composition was the most cited paper (42 citations). After 2018, his research shifted towards metabolic management during the dairy cow transition period and the immunomodulatory effects of plant extracts.
In terms of overall academic influence, this researcher is a stable contributor to regional practical agricultural science rather than a global leader. With an h-index of 11 and 479 total citations, however, the number of citations in the last 5 years was only 31, indicating that his academic influence was mainly concentrated from 2003 to 2013, with significant decline in recent years. His publications are primarily in Turkish and Eastern European professional journals, such as Research in Veterinary Science and Meat Science.
⚠️ Important Data Quality Note: There is a clear confusion regarding the names of some papers. Papers related to AISI 4042 steel welding microstructure (2010), ethyl acetate reaction distillation simulations (2013, 2022), and brine purification (2018) do not correspond to his animal science background. It is likely that these papers were incorrectly classified as belonging to the same researcher due to name confusion. These papers are not included in the academic analysis.
Breakthrough Works 1. Use of Ultrasound to Predict Carcass Composition of Live Akkaraman Lambs (2008) Description: Using real-time ultrasound scanning technology, this study predicts the carcass composition of live Akkaraman sheep after slaughtering, including muscle thickness, fat thickness, and eye muscle area. This method avoids the limitations of traditional slaughter methods, providing a non-invasive tool for breeding selection and product quality prediction.
Why couldn’t it be done before: Ultrasound equipment was bulky and expensive in the early days. Portable real-time ultrasound instruments became popular in animal science only in the early 2000s, and it required a large amount of data on breed-specific correction equations. There was no available data on breed-specific corrections for Turkish local breeds like Akkaraman, which limited the implementation of this technology.
Impact: This paper is one of the most cited papers by this researcher (42 citations). It has promoted the use of ultrasound technology in sheep breeding and production management in the Middle East and Central Asia. It provides a methodological reference for evaluating the quality of live animals.
2. Effects of Probiotics: Alone and in a Mixture of Biosacc plus Zinc Bacitracin on the Caecal Microflora of Japanese Quails (2003) Description: This study systematically compared the effects of probiotics alone and probiotics combined with zinc bacitracin on the intestinal microflora of quails. It explored the possibility of using probiotics as a substitute for antibiotic growth promoters.
Why couldn’t it be done before: The EU’s ban on antibiotics in feedstuffs began in the early 2000s, which forced the industry to seek scientific evidence for alternative solutions. There was little quantitative research on probiotics’ effects on quail intestinal microflora, and microbial detection techniques (traditional culture methods) were only beginning to be used in poultry nutrition research.
Impact: This paper has been cited 35 times. It provided early experimental evidence for the use of herbal additives as a substitute for antibiotics. It is valuable in the literature on antibiotic alternatives.
3. Stress Responses to Comparative Handling Procedures in Sheep (2013) Description: This study systematically compared the effects of various routine management operations such as milking, shearing, and weighing on stress physiological parameters like cortisol levels and blood biochemical indicators in sheep. It quantified the relative impact of different operations on animal welfare.
Why couldn’t it be done before: The scientific evaluation framework for animal welfare was only gradually established in Europe during the mid-2000s. Standardized methods for measuring welfare indicators, such as cortisol measurements, became operational at the small and medium-sized farm level during this period. Previous research was often isolated measurements of individual operations, lacking systematic comparisons across different types of operations.
Impact: This paper has been cited 29 times. It provides an empirical basis for comparing multiple operations in sheep welfare evaluation. It is valuable in animal welfare science and ranch management practices.
4. Effects of Aniseed (Pimpinella anisum L.) on Egg Production, Quality, Cholesterol Levels, Hatching Results, and Antibody Values in Blood of Laying Quails (2007) Description: This study systematically evaluated the effects of fennel as a natural feed additive on quail egg production, egg quality, serum cholesterol levels, hatching results, and antibody values in blood. It integrated the multi-dimensional functions of plant essential oils/extracts into a single research framework.
Why couldn’t it be done before: The EU’s ban on antibiotics in feedstuffs began in 2003, which led to a demand for systematic research on alternative natural additives. At the same time, the identification and quantification of essential oil active components (such as fennel oil) became more advanced in the early 2000s. This made it possible to study the effectiveness of plant additives.
Impact: This paper has been cited 24 times. It is one of the important early papers on the use of herbal plants in poultry production. It provided a methodological template for many subsequent studies on similar topics, such as oregano, mint, and pomegranate.
5. Effects of Short Chain Fatty Acid (SCFA) Supplementation on Performance and Egg Characteristics of Old Breeder Hens (2007) Description: This study explored the effects of short chain fatty acids (such as propionic acid and butyric acid) on the egg production performance and egg quality of old breeding hens. It proposed operational nutritional interventions for groups of breeding hens in the final stage of their lives.
Why couldn’t it be done before: The role of short chain fatty acids in intestinal health and mucosal integrity was only gradually understood in the field of monogastric animal nutrition in the early 2000s. Previous research on SCFA mainly focused on piglets, and its application in older poultry breeding hens was still unknown. The stability issues of commercial products containing SCFA were also addressed during this period.
Impact: This paper has been cited 21 times. It provided a non-antibiotic solution for maintaining intestinal health in old breeding hens. It is valuable in the literature on breeding hen management.
Research Areas Natural herbal plants and extracts as feed additives for poultry/ruminants (oregano, fennel, poppy seeds, mint, pomegranate, myrtle) Optimization of poultry production performance (egg production rate, egg quality, growth performance of laying hens and broilers) Nutrition and health management of ruminants (prediction of dairy cow carcass composition, metabolic management during dairy cow transition period, milk production of buffalo) Probiotics and intestinal microbial regulation as a substitute for antibiotics Animal welfare and stress physiological assessment Application of near-infrared spectroscopy in rapid analysis of feed components Methodological Evolution The research methodology has evolved through three clear stages. In the early stage (1999-2006), traditional feeding experiments were used, with measurement indicators limited to basic production performance (body weight gain, feed conversion rate) and conventional blood biochemical parameters. The experimental design was relatively simple, and the sample size was small. In the middle stage (2007-2013), the methodology became more comprehensive: ultrasound imaging technology was introduced for non-destructive prediction of carcass composition, antioxidant enzyme activity (SOD, CAT, MDA) and lipid peroxidation indicators were added, a factorial design for multi-factor composite treatments was established, and蛋黄脂肪酸谱分析 was included in the evaluation system. The research dimension expanded from single production performance to food quality and safety. In the late stage (2015-present), near-infrared spectroscopy was used to establish quantitative calibration models for rapid feed quality testing. Metabolomics indicators such as ketosis biomarkers and acute-phase proteins were introduced into dairy cow studies, and the DAISY Incubator was also used to compare digestive characteristics of multiple species. Overall, the evolution of methodology is a follow-up adoption of industry technology rather than original development of cutting-edge methods.
Domain Impact Ismail Bayram’s contributions to animal nutrition are part of regional practical scientific accumulation rather than paradigm-shifting impacts. His core value lies in establishing a wealth of localized data on natural feed additives for common breeds in Turkey and surrounding areas, filling the gap in international literature that neglected these local livestock breeds. Under the EU policy banning antibiotics in feedstuffs, he continues to provide empirical evidence for herbal alternatives. However, his overall influence is limited: only 31 citations in the last 5 years indicate a significant decline in academic influence. His publications lack high-impact factor journals, and the scalability of his research results is limited by specific breeds and geographical regions. His research serves the production needs of Turkish animal husbandry rather than pushing the theoretical frontiers of animal nutrition.

Jiachen Mao — Researcher Analysis Report Analysis Mode: fast | Analysis Time: 2026-03-16T00:50:32
Rating: Active Researcher (33.0/100) Basic Metrics Metric Value Institution Unknown h-index 11 Total Citations 616 Citations in Last 5 Years 14 Number of Papers 23 Papers Published in Top Journals 0 Publication Period 2015 - 2025 Semantic Scholar 3384503 Research Trajectory Jiachen Mao’s research trajectory is extremely rare, characterized by drastic transitions across different fields. It’s difficult to describe using the conventional “field expert/rising star” framework. From 2015 to 2018, his contributions were mainly in the areas of building energy simulation and urban microclimate. He published several highly cited papers on ventilation in high-rise buildings, energy calibration optimization, and urban heat island effect analysis, laying the foundation for his early academic reputation (h-index=11, with total citations of 616 all from this period).
In 2019, there was a sudden shift: he began to publish papers on deep learning acceleration hardware (DASNet, HyPar), which had nothing to do with the building field. After that, his research became more dispersed: in 2020, he turned to power systems and energy storage; in 2022, he focused on fire protection of concrete structures. From 2023 to 2026, he published papers on LLM safety (SafeWork-R1, SafeCoT), recommendation system embedding optimization, diffusion model adversarial attacks, reviews on zeolite synthesis, phonetics, etc., all of which are unrelated fields.
This highly dispersed publication record (with only 14 citations in the last 5 years) strongly suggests that this ‘Jiachen Mao’ is actually a combination of multiple researchers with the same name in the database, rather than an individual’s personal trajectory. If treated as a single researcher, his work in the early building environment field was influential, but his recent output lacks focus and he hasn’t established a continuous academic position in any single field.
Breakthrough Works 1. A low cost seasonal solar soil heat storage system for greenhouse heating: Design and pilot study (2015) Description: Described and tested a low-cost seasonal solar soil heat storage system for winter heating in greenhouses. This system stores solar energy in the soil during summer and utilizes it during winter, enabling thermal transfer across seasons.
Why couldn’t it be done before: Seasonal soil heat storage was difficult to implement due to its complexity and high cost. This paper proposed a simplified design and conducted field tests, reducing the engineering barriers. Without a verified low-cost prototype, the feasibility of this method was uncertain.
Impact: Became a highly cited reference in this field (125 citations), providing an economical technical approach for low-carbon heating in agricultural greenhouses. It has been widely referenced in subsequent research on energy storage and agricultural energy systems.
2. An automated optimization method for calibrating building energy simulation models with measured data: Orientation and a case study (2016) Description: Proposed an automated optimization method for calibrating building energy simulation models using measured data, solving the problem of discrepancies between simulation results and real-world energy consumption. This method automates the manual calibration process.
Why couldn’t it be done before: Building energy simulation models have many parameters (material thermal properties, equipment efficiency, behavior of users, etc.). Traditional manual calibration relied on engineers’ experience and was very time-consuming. Automated optimization algorithms were not yet mature in building simulation before 2016, and the computational cost limited large-scale parameter searches.
Impact: Had 87 citations, becoming an important reference for modeling methods in building energy simulation. It promoted automated calibration practices in digital twins and Building Information Modeling (BIM) fields.
3. Global sensitivity analysis of an urban microclimate system under uncertainty: Design and case study (2017) Description: Systematically introduced global sensitivity analysis into urban microclimate simulations, quantifying the contribution of various input parameters to the uncertainty in urban thermal environment predictions and identifying key influencing factors.
Why couldn’t it be done before: Urban microclimate model parameters were highly dimensional (building geometry, materials, vegetation, meteorology, etc.). Local sensitivity analysis couldn’t capture the interaction effects between parameters. Calculating global sensitivity analysis using Sobol’s method was extremely costly until high-performance computing became widespread in 2017.
Impact: Had 76 citations, providing a methodological framework for quantifying uncertainties in urban climate models. It promoted the evaluation of model credibility in climate-adaptive urban planning.
4. The airborne transmission of infection between flats in high-rise residential buildings: A review (2015) Description: Systematically reviewed the mechanisms of droplet and aerosol transmission between floors in high-rise residential buildings, focusing on cross-floor交叉感染 pathways driven by单侧自然通风. Public health risks were also assessed.
Why couldn’t it be done before: Cross-floor aerosol transmission became a concern after SARS in 2003, but relevant experiments and simulation data didn’t accumulate until 2015 to support a systematic review. High-rise dense living patterns are unique to East Asia, so Western literature coverage was insufficient.
Impact: Had 61 citations, being widely referenced during the COVID-19 pandemic. It became an important reference for evaluating infection risks in high-rise buildings and influenced discussions on ventilation design guidelines.
5. Planning Low-carbon Distributed Power Systems: Evaluating the Role of Energy Storage (2020) Description: Incorporated energy storage systems (with dynamic charge-discharge efficiency models) into power capacity expansion planning frameworks, evaluating the economic and technical value of energy storage in decarbonizing distributed power systems.
Why couldn’t it be done before: Early capacity planning models simplified energy storage as static devices, ignoring the dynamic behavior of charge-discharge efficiency with state changes. Lithium batteries hadn’t seen a significant cost reduction until 2020, so the commercial significance of incorporating energy storage into planning models was limited. With rapidly declining energy storage costs, the need for accurate modeling became urgent.
Impact: Had 46 citations, providing a tool for power system planners to quantify energy storage value. It has some application value in decision-making related to renewable energy absorption and grid decarbonization.
Research Directions Simulation, calibration, and optimization of building and urban energy systems (2015-2021) AI model safety, alignment of large language models, and safety of visual language models (2024-2026, current focus) Efficiency of deep learning systems and hardware acceleration (2019, brief involvement) Renewable energy and energy storage planning (2020, single project) Methodological Evolution From 2015 to 2018, the research method was centered around physical simulation: CFD fluid dynamics, building energy simulation engines (such as EnergyPlus), combined with optimization algorithms (genetic algorithms, Bayesian optimization) for calibration and sensitivity analysis. This was a classic approach in computational engineering. Starting from 2019, deep learning methods (sparse activation, parallel training) emerged, indicating a fundamental shift in the methodological toolbox. In 2024-2025, AI safety work adopted contemporary LLM alignment techniques such as reinforcement learning-based training and thought-chain supervision, completely breaking away from early building physics methods.
This evolution isn’t organic; it’s more like the result of researchers with different backgrounds being aggregated in the database. If considered as a single person, their methodological shift is much faster than normal academic transition, lacking transitional work as a bridge.
Domain Impact This researcher (or group of researchers with the same name) has made substantial contributions to the field of building environment engineering: three papers from 2015-2017 had more than 50 citations each, leaving a referenceable methodological foundation in subfields such as urban microclimate simulation, high-rise building ventilation and infectious disease risks, and automatic building energy calibration. However, only 14 citations in the last 5 years indicate that they haven’t established influence in new areas like AI, and no single direction has been continuously developed. Overall, this is a scholar with moderate influence in a single field (building energy), highly dispersed recently, and lacking top journal publications. They don’t have cross-field integration potential.

content:
Jie Guo — Researcher Analysis Report Analysis Mode: fast | Analysis Time: 2026-03-16T00:52:17
Rating: Active Researcher (33.4/100) Basic Metrics Metric Value Institution Unknown h-index 11 Total Citations 2,266 Citations in Last 5 Years 0 Number of Papers 15 Papers Published in Top Journals 0 Publication Period 2000 - 2021 Semantic Scholar 50115592 Research Trajectory It should be noted that this is not the academic trajectory of a single researcher, but rather a result of data aggregation errors for multiple researchers with the same name (Guo Jie/Guo Jie). The high-citation papers from 2000-2005 (with over 2,000 citations in total) were contributed by a public health researcher specializing in teen substance abuse and social development, published in top public health journals such as American Journal of Public Health and Journal of Studies on Alcohol. From 2016-2018, there was a sudden surge in papers related to thermal conductivity materials science. Since 2017, the research shifted towards computer vision (scene text detection, object tracking). After 2019, the papers covered unrelated fields such as astronomy, 3D Gaussian sputtering, quantum physics, dance generation, and recommendation systems. Such a span is impossible for a single researcher.
Therefore, the data with an h-index of 11 and total citations of 2266 actually comes from at least two different researchers: one was a public health scholar active from 2000-2005, who contributed most of the citations, and another (or multiple) were computer science/materials science researchers active after 2016. The anomaly of having zero citations in the last 5 years also confirms that there was an error in data aggregation.
Breakthrough Works 1. The dynamics of alcohol and marijuana initiation: patterns and predictors of first use in adolescence (2000) Description: Using longitudinal tracking data, this study analyzed the timing and predictors of adolescents’ first use of alcohol and marijuana, revealing the developmental patterns of substance abuse initiation.
Why it couldn’t be done before: It required large-scale longitudinal cohort data and a long tracking period (spanning adolescence). Such data became available only in the late 1990s. Additionally, multi-level statistical modeling methods were needed to handle repeated measurements.
Impact: With 444 citations, this paper became a classic literature on adolescent substance abuse initiation, providing a framework of key predictors for subsequent intervention studies.
2. Long-Term Effects of the Seattle Social Development Intervention on School Bonding Trajectories (2001) Description: This study evaluated the long-term effects of the Seattle social development intervention project, tracking the development of school belonging and proving the lasting impact of early social interventions.
Why it couldn’t be done before: Interventional studies require a follow-up period of over 10 years to assess long-term effects. Data collection was costly, and controlling for dropout rates was difficult. Such large-scale longitudinal interventions were only supported by funding in the 1990s.
Impact: With 416 citations, this paper laid an empirical foundation for early intervention effectiveness in the field of prevention science, influencing adolescent health policies for decades.
3. Developmental relationships between adolescent substance use and risky sexual behavior in young adulthood (2002) Description: From a developmental psychology perspective, this study revealed the longitudinal relationship between adolescent substance use and high-risk sexual behavior in early adulthood, proposing a common development path hypothesis.
Why it couldn’t be done before: It required long-term tracking data from adolescence to early adulthood, with measurements of multiple risk behaviors. The methodology for analyzing such multi-risk behavior combinations (such as latent variable growth curve models) became more mature only in the early 2000s.
Impact: With 298 citations, this paper effectively connected substance abuse research with sexual health research, promoting the development of integrated adolescent risk behavior intervention strategies.
4. Family influences on the risk of daily smoking initiation (2005) Description: This study systematically analyzed the impact of family factors (parental smoking behavior, family supervision, quality parent-child relationships) on adolescents’ risk of starting to smoke daily.
Why it couldn’t be done before: It required collecting matching data reported by teenagers and their parents simultaneously, while controlling for genetic confounding factors. The widespread use of survival analysis methods in epidemiology was a technical prerequisite.
Impact: With 289 citations, this paper directly influenced the design of tobacco control intervention projects at the family level and was widely cited in tobacco control policy research.
5. Superior thermal conductivity in suspended bilayer hexagonal boron nitride (2016) Description: This study measured the surface thermal conductivity of suspended bilayer hexagonal boron nitride using pre-patterned microstructures, revealing its extremely high thermal conductivity properties.
Why it couldn’t be done before: It required dry transfer technology to prepare high-quality suspended h-BN samples (reducing polymer residues) and a precise thermal measurement platform for ultra-thin two-dimensional materials. These technologies became available around 2015.
Impact: This study opened up new research directions for hexagonal boron nitride as a thermal management material, providing an experimental basis for understanding the physics of heat transfer in two-dimensional materials.
Research Directions Adolescent substance abuse and risk behavior development (public health/prevention science, 2000-2005) Thermal conductivity of two-dimensional materials and nanoscale heat transfer (material physics, 2016-2018) Computer vision and 3D content generation (scene text detection, object tracking, 3D Gaussian sputtering, dance generation, 2017-2026) Multimodal recommendation systems and natural language processing (2022-2026) Quantum information and quantum entanglement measurement (2024-2025) Methodological Evolution Since the data of multiple researchers with the same name were aggregated in this file system, it is impossible to track a coherent methodological evolution. For the public health researcher (2000-2005), their methodology started from descriptive epidemiology and gradually evolved into longitudinal tracking designs, latent variable growth curve models, and survival analysis, reflecting the mature development of prevention science methodologies. For the group of computer vision researchers (2017-2026), a typical deep learning methodology evolution can be observed: CNN feature extraction (2017-2018) → attention mechanism (2018-2019) → Transformer architecture (2021-2022) → diffusion models and 3D Gaussian sputtering (2023-2026). This corresponds to the technological iteration of the entire field.
There is no logical connection between these two methodological threads, further confirming the existence of name aggregation problems.
Domain Impact When evaluated as an independent individual, “Jie Guo” in the public health field had substantial academic influence: 5 core papers with over 2,000 citations, many published in top journals. He was a reliable and influential researcher in the field of adolescent prevention science. As for “Jie Guo” in the computer vision/materials science field (2016-2026), although he wrote many papers, his citations per paper were limited. He was not a leader but rather someone who followed the mainstream technological trends. The h-index of 11 in this entire file mainly came from public health papers, and the fact that there were zero citations in the last 5 years indicates that public health researchers have stopped publishing. It is recommended to re-evaluate using either institution affiliation or ORCID to distinguish between researchers with the same name.

Jun Lu — Research Analyst Report Analysis Mode: fast | Analysis Time: 2026-03-16T00:14:36
Rating: New Star (57.2/100) Basic Metrics Metric Value Institution Unknown h-index 27 Total Citations 2,852 Citations in Last 5 Years 186 Number of Papers 207 Papers Published in Top Journals 13 Publication Period 1989 - 2023 Semantic Scholar 5223928 Research Trajectory Jun Lu’s academic trajectory shows a clear two-phase evolution. The first phase (1989–2013) focused on numerical calculations of electromagnetic fields, particularly the Harmonic Balance Finite-Element Method (HB-FEM) and the design of high-frequency planar transformers. This period laid a solid theoretical foundation in the analysis of nonlinear magnetic fields. A key turning point occurred around 2013–2015: with the rise of the global electric vehicle industry and smart grid policies, his research on high-frequency magnetic devices and wireless charging became highly valuable from an industrial perspective. As a result, his research shifted towards EV wireless charging, microgrid energy management, and optimization of energy storage systems. The second phase (2014–2023) reached its peak in 2018 with a comprehensive review paper titled “366 Citations”. This work established Jun Lu as a leading scholar in the field of EV wireless charging. It is worth noting that papers published after 2022, such as those on superconducting materials, Riemannian geometry, quantum networks, and insect-computer hybrid speakers, show severe author data contamination. These works should be considered as contributions by different researchers rather than part of Jun Lu’s academic trajectory.
Breakthrough Works 1. Analysis of the DC Bias Phenomenon by the Harmonic Balance Finite-Element Method (2011) Description: Applied harmonic balance finite element method to analyze the DC bias phenomenon in transformers, establishing a complete mathematical model that can accurately calculate harmonic distributions in nonlinear saturated magnetic fields.
Why it couldn’t be done before: The magnetic saturation caused by DC bias is a highly nonlinear problem. Traditional time-domain finite element methods require excessive computational resources and are difficult to converge. The HB-FEM theory was not yet mature at that time, lacking fixed-point iteration algorithms for handling non-linear hysteresis curves. Moreover, computer performance was insufficient to support three-dimensional harmonic solutions.
Impact: Provided an authoritative simulation tool for the design and protection of DC bias in power system transformers. Published in IEEE Transactions on Power Delivery, with 51 citations, this work became a benchmark document in this field.
2. Characterizations of High Frequency Planar Transformer With a Novel Comb-Shaped Shield (2011) Description: Proposed a comb-shaped shielding structure for high-frequency planar transformers, systematically characterizing their electromagnetic shielding efficiency and eddy current loss characteristics. This provided a new structural solution for on-chip power supplies and wireless charging couplers.
Why it couldn’t be done before: The skin effect and proximity effect were complex in high frequencies, making traditional analytical models ineffective. Fine three-dimensional finite element simulations combined with experimental verification were needed, but such simulation tools and micro-manufacturing processes were not widely available in the early 2000s.
Impact: With 45 citations, this work promoted the evolution of planar transformers towards higher frequencies and power densities. It laid the foundation for research on integrated magnetic devices in subsequent LLC resonant converters.
3. Autoregressive with Exogenous Variables and Neural Network Short-Term Load Forecast Models for Residential Low Voltage Distribution Networks (2014) Description: Developed a hybrid ARIMAX and neural network model for short-term load forecasting in residential low-voltage distribution networks. This model identified key exogenous variables that affect demand, enabling accurate predictions of total electricity consumption and peak demand the next day.
Why it couldn’t be done before: The behavior of end-users in low-voltage distribution networks was highly random. Traditional statistical models had insufficient generalization capabilities. Deep learning hadn’t become widely used at that time, and early neural networks faced overfitting and training difficulties. With the widespread adoption of smart meters, high-resolution electricity data became available for the first time, making data-driven modeling possible.
Impact: With 80 citations, this work became an important benchmark in the field of distribution network demand forecasting. It directly inspired research on prediction modules in microgrid energy management systems.
4. Review of static and dynamic wireless electric vehicle charging system (2018) Description: Conducted a systematic review of static and dynamic wireless electric vehicle charging technologies, covering induction coupling principles, coil design, power electronics topologies, interoperability standards, and safety aspects. A technology roadmap was proposed.
Why it couldn’t be done before: This review was possible because there was a wealth of original research on wireless charging technology from 2012 to 2018, which led to its industrialization. The development of Qi standards and the SAE J2954 draft made standardization discussions possible. Jun Lu’s ten years of experience in planar transformers, induction couplers, and EV charging systems allowed him to conduct this authoritative review.
Impact: With 366 citations, this work was the most influential among all papers in his research list. It established Jun Lu as a leading scholar in the field of EV wireless charging, with many subsequent studies citing it as a reference.
5. Short-term load forecasting for microgrid energy management system using hybrid HHO-FNN model with best-basis stationary wavelet packet transform (2020) Description: Applied optimal basis stationary wavelet packet transformation to decompose load signals, and combined with Harris-Ho optimization algorithm for parameter tuning of feedforward neural networks. This created a dedicated short-term load forecasting framework for microgrid energy management.
Why it couldn’t be done before: The Harris-Ho algorithm was introduced in 2019, making it a relatively new meta-heuristic optimization tool at that time. Hybrid prediction frameworks combining deep learning with intelligent optimization algorithms required sufficient computational resources and standardized microgrid operation datasets. Neither of these existed before 2019.
Impact: With 105 citations, this work was Jun Lu’s most influential original research in the field of load forecasting and energy management. It established a research paradigm for “signal decomposition + meta-heuristic optimization + neural network” in the microgrid domain.
Research Areas Harmonic Balance Finite-Element Method (HB-FEM) and nonlinear electromagnetic field analysis Design of high-frequency planar transformers and integrated magnetic devices Static and dynamic wireless charging technologies for electric vehicles Microgrid energy management, energy storage optimization, and control Short-term load forecasting and wind power prediction (data-driven methods) Smart grids and Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) systems Methodological Evolution Jun Lu’s methodology has undergone three paradigm shifts. The first phase (1989–2012) focused on mathematical analysis: the harmonic balance method transformed nonlinear differential equations into complex algebraic systems, which were then discretized using finite element methods to form the unique computational electromagnetics toolchain of HB-FEM. The evolution of this method progressed from simple harmonic truncation to fixed-point iteration and neural network-assisted hysteresis modeling. The second phase (2013–2018) shifted towards system-level modeling and control: the research scope expanded from magnetic devices to power electronics topologies and microgrid hierarchical control architectures. The methodology evolved from numerical simulations to experimental platform verification and hardware-in-the-loop testing. MEMS radio frequency switches and wireless charging couplers were also designed with a focus on micro-manufacturing processes. The third phase (2019–2023) fully introduced a data-driven approach: combining wavelet packet decomposition, meta-heuristic optimization (HHO, GWO, SSA), and deep neural networks (LSTM). This reflected the trend of shifting research in energy systems towards machine learning methods. Jun Lu effectively applied his signal processing experience to time-series forecasting problems, maintaining methodological consistency.
Domain Impact Jun Lu’s contributions to the field are evident at two levels: At the foundational method level, his HB-FEM work (1989–2017) provided a mature computational tool for analyzing DC bias in power transformers and high-frequency nonlinear magnetic fields. His findings were published in top journals such as IEEE Transactions on Magnetics and IEEE Transactions on Power Delivery, making his work valuable for academic reference. At the applied level, his 2018 review paper on EV wireless charging (366 citations) became a essential reference for newcomers to this field, effectively driving research agenda setting. Overall, Jun Lu is a leading scholar focused on engineering applications: with an h-index of 27 and approximately 2852 total citations, he has established a solid academic influence in the intersection of power electronics and smart grids. However, his papers published in top journals are only 13, and his citations in the last five years (186) are relatively limited, indicating that his peak period has passed. His current influence primarily relies on long-tail citations accumulated over time.

summary: Jun Lu: Jun Lu’s academic career can be divided into two distinct phases. The first phase (1989–2013) was dedicated to numerical calculations of electromagnetic fields, with a particular focus on the Harmonic Balance Finite Element Method and the design of high-frequency planar transformers. During this period, he established a solid theoretical foundation in the field of nonlinear magnetic field analysis. The key turning point occurred around 2013–2015.

L. Teng — Researcher Analysis Report Analysis Mode: fast | Analysis Time: 2026-03-16T00:17:54
Rating: Active Researcher (45.5/100) Basic Metrics Metric Value Institution Unknown h-index 21 Total Citations 1,607 Recent 5-year Citations 63 Number of Papers 55 Papers in Top Journals 0 Years of Publication 2008 - 2025 Semantic Scholar 49286689 Research Trajectory L. Teng’s academic trajectory is highly fragmented, with papers covering a wide range of fields including glioma treatment, cardiac stem cells, NADPH oxidase vascular biology, immunology in marine model organisms, and spin relaxation in GaAs quantum wells—all areas that are not related to each other. This strongly suggests that this researcher identity is actually the result of collaboration between multiple researchers (a problem of merging with the same name). If we focus on his main research lines, we can identify a research entity centered around glioma treatment and diagnosis: In 2011, he published a review article titled “Aberrant Signaling Pathways in Glioma” which received 219 citations. Between 2011 and 2013, he published several influential papers on mechanisms related to 5-ALA fluorescence-guided surgery and photodynamic therapy, which constituted the main source of his academic influence.
The key turning point in his career was during the intensive period from 2011 to 2013—through revealing the regulatory mechanism between iron chelators and ABCB6 overexpression, he provided a molecular target for improving the quality of clinical 5-ALA fluorescence surgery. His three representative papers received over 450 citations. However, his citations in the past five years were only 63 (out of 1,607 lifetime citations), indicating that his research influence has significantly declined after 2018. His new directions are becoming more dispersed (C-phycocyanin, zebrafish, SGLT-2), and he has not established a continuous core research program.
Breakthrough Works 1. Aberrant Signaling Pathways in Glioma (2011) Description: A systematic review of the abnormal activation mechanisms of key signaling pathways such as EGFR, PDGFR, PTEN/PI3K/Akt, Rb/CDK4, and p53 in glioblastoma multiforme, providing a theoretical framework for targeted treatment strategies.
Why couldn’t it be done before: The accumulation of key insights required time: After the release of TCGA glioma genome data in 2007, researchers were able to systematically integrate the full picture of multi-pathway abnormalities. Prior research on each pathway was relatively isolated, lacking a cross-pathway integration perspective.
Impact: It became a highly cited review in the field of glioma signaling pathways (219 citations), providing a standard reference framework for subsequent targeted drug development and combination therapy strategies.
2. Silencing of ferrochelatase enhances 5-aminolevulinic acid-based fluorescence and photodynamic therapy efficacy (2011) Description: It was found that silencing iron chelators could block the metabolism from PpIX to hemoglobin, thereby significantly enhancing the fluorescence intensity of 5-ALA in glioma cells and improving the effectiveness of photodynamic therapy.
Why couldn’t it be done before: Clinical 5-ALA fluorescence surgery has been used for many years, but insufficient PpIX accumulation limited the quality of fluorescence. The breakthrough of this work was identifying FECH as a key metabolic bottleneck—both detailed enzyme maps of the 5-ALA metabolism pathway and mature siRNA technology were developed around 2010.
Impact: It received 158 citations, pioneering the research direction of improving 5-ALA fluorescence quality through metabolic intervention, providing an operational target for optimizing clinical fluorescence-guided surgery.
3. Increased Expression of ABCB6 Enhances Protoporphyrin IX Accumulation and Photodynamic Effect in Human Glioma (2013) Description: It was revealed that overexpression of ATP-binding cassette transporter ABCB6 could promote the accumulation of PpIX in mitochondria, enhancing the sensitivity of glioma to photodynamic therapy from another mechanism.
Why couldn’t it be done before: The function of ABCB6 in porphyrin transport was only initially understood in 2009. Connecting it with the enhancement of glioma PDT required cross-disciplinary knowledge integration (mitochondrial transport biology + tumor photodynamic therapy), which had not been completed before.
Impact: It received 98 citations, together with iron chelators research, constructing a dual-target strategy for regulating PpIX metabolism, driving a wave of research on the mechanism of glioma photodynamic therapy.
4. The Heme Oxygenase 1 Inducer (CoPP) Protects Human Cardiac Stem Cells against Apoptosis through Activation of the Extracellular Signal-regulated Kinase (ERK)/NRF2 Signaling Pathway and Cytokine Release (2012) Description: It was demonstrated for the first time that HO-1 inducer CoPP could enhance the survival rate of human cardiac stem cells after transplantation by activating the ERK/NRF2 pathway and cytokine release. This solved the core problem of high cell death rates during stem cell transplantation in clinical treatment.
Why couldn’t it be done before: The clinical potential of c-kit+ cardiac stem cells was only confirmed in 2003, and their extremely low survival rate (more than 90% died within 7 days) became a bottleneck. Applying the cellular protection mechanism of HO-1/CO system to cardiac stem cell pre-treatment required systematic integration of knowledge from two independent fields (HO-1 cardiovascular protection and stem cell therapy).
Impact: It received 97 citations, establishing the paradigm of “preparation + stem cell transplantation” combination strategy, promoting research on cell survival in cardiac regeneration medicine.
5. The synergistic antitumor effects of all-trans retinoic acid and C-phycocyanin on the lung cancer A549 cells in vitro and in vivo (2015) Description: In both in vitro and in vivo models, it was confirmed that the combined use of all-trans retinoic acid and C-phycocyanin had a synergistic antitumor effect on lung cancer A549 cells, providing experimental evidence for the combination of natural products with differentiation induction therapy.
Why couldn’t it be done before: The anti-tumor mechanism of C-phycocyanin was gradually understood in the early 2010s. Combining it with mature ATRA differentiation treatment required cross-structural research on both mechanisms, and data accumulation was only sufficient for systematic combined research around 2015.
Impact: It received 86 citations, making it one of the landmark studies in the field of C-phycocyanin anti-tumor applications, promoting the exploration of this natural pigment in tumor pharmacology.
Research Directions Glioma treatment and diagnosis (optimization of 5-ALA fluorescence-guided surgery mechanisms, sensitization of photodynamic therapy, targeted signaling therapy, glioma stem cells) Cardiac stem cells and myocardial protection (HO-1/NO pre-treatment strategies, ERK/NRF2 survival pathways) NADPH oxidase and endothelial cell oxidative stress (regulation mechanism of p47phox phosphorylation) Marine/Zebrafish model organism immunology and developmental biology (defensins, Ly6 family, MOV10, CD248) Methodological Evolution Early stages (2008-2010) relied mainly on classical molecular biology methods: western blot, gene knockout mouse models, protein phosphorylation analysis, focusing primarily on the regulatory mechanism of NADPH oxidase. From 2011 to 2014, methodology matured with the introduction of RNAi gene silencing, fluorescence microscopy imaging, and in vitro photodynamic therapy models. Experimental design shifted from single-molecule mechanisms to a full-chain verification of “target → intervention → functional phenotype,” leading to the production of the most influential papers. After 2015, methodology became significantly fragmented: on one hand, in vivo tumor models continued; on the other hand, completely different methods such as zebrafish developmental models, systematic developmental analysis, and reproductive toxicology testing were introduced, indicating a lack of continuous focus in research directions. In recent years (2024-2025), clinical observational studies have been pursued, with cardiac clinical reviews published, showing little methodological continuity compared to early basic research.
Domain Impact In the sub-field of glioma 5-ALA metabolism regulation, L. Teng made substantial contributions as the main researcher in glioma research: Through the discovery of two complementary mechanism targets, iron chelators and ABCB6, he systematically clarified the metabolic network that affects PpIX accumulation, which was frequently cited by subsequent research in this field. His h-index of 21 mainly came from this period. However, due to the loss of focus on his main research lines after 2017, he failed to establish a continuous research program around 5-ALA surgery optimization or glioma targeted therapy, missing the window to advance early mechanism discoveries into clinical translation. Overall assessment: He is an effective contributor in specific sub-fields, not a leading figure in the overall domain; his significant decline in influence over the past five years (only 63 citations) indicates that his academic influence has entered a plateau phase.
