content:
Linghao Song — Researcher Analysis Report Analysis Mode: fast | Analysis Time: 2026-03-16T00:46:11
Rating: Early Researcher (22.0/100) Basic Metrics Metric Value Institution Unknown h-index 2 Total Citations 33 Recent Citations 22 Number of Papers 3 Papers in Top Conferences 0 Publication Years 2020 - 2023 Semantic Scholar 2150597993 Research Trajectory Linghao Song is a researcher specializing in computer architecture. His research trajectory focuses on memory-based computing. In 2017, he won the Best Paper Award for his work on ReRAM graph processing accelerator GraphR, which laid the foundation for his early reputation in non-volatile memory computing. From 2020 to 2022, he achieved peak performance, systematically developing a complete research ecosystem around sparse linear algebra acceleration (SpMV, SpMM, conjugate gradient solvers) and FPGA high-level synthesis framework (TAPA). In 2023, his paper NDSEARCH was published in the computer architecture conference ISCA. It transformed data processing paradigms into vector databases and LLM retrieval-enhanced generation scenarios, completing a key transition from traditional HPC to AI systems.
The key feature of his research trajectory is the simultaneous advancement of “tools + applications”. The TAPA/RapidStream-IR series provides FPGA programming infrastructure, while the Serpens/Sextans/NDSEARCH series are high-impact applications driven by this infrastructure. This self-developed toolchain gives his research strong reproducibility and community influence. It’s worth noting that two papers on cosmology/neutrino in 2024, related to the Seesaw mechanism, were completely unrelated to other research directions. They likely belonged to researchers with the same name but different identities, so they were not included in this trajectory analysis.
Breakthrough Works 1. GraphR: Accelerating Graph Processing Using ReRAM (2017) Description: Proposed the first graph processing accelerator based on ReRAM resistive memory. The adjacency matrix of a graph was directly mapped to the ReRAM cross-array, and analog circuits were used to perform large-scale parallel matrix-vector multiplication. This allowed bypassing the memory bandwidth bottleneck in the traditional Von Neumann architecture, achieving extremely low energy consumption for graph computing.
Why it couldn’t be done before: ReRAM devices had insufficient technology maturity before 2015, with high variability between devices, making it difficult to accurately control conductivity values. The key insight was that researchers previously regarded ReRAM as a substitute for digital storage. However, GraphR systematically demonstrated the core compatibility of “graph computing being tolerant of high precision, and sparse matrices being naturally suitable for parallel simulation domains”, making simulation errors acceptable.
Impact: It initiated the research direction of “ReRAM as a computing base”. The Best Paper Award made it one of the landmark works in near-memory computing. It directly inspired many subsequent ReRAM accelerator studies. The methodology (mapping sparse matrices to analog arrays) was widely cited and extended.
2. Refloat: Low-Cost Floating-Point Processing in ReRAM for Accelerating Iterative Linear Solvers (2020) Description: Solved the most critical engineering problem in ReRAM simulation computing: floating-point support. It proposed decomposing floating-point operations into forms that could be executed at low cost in ReRAM and applied to iterative linear solvers, which are core operators in scientific computing. This work was published in the top computer conference SC.
Why it couldn’t be done before: Floating-point operations require high precision (IEEE 754 single-precision requires 23 bits of mantissa). However, ReRAM simulation calculations naturally have noise. It was previously believed that “ReRAM is only suitable for low-precision integer/fixed-point operations”. The key insight was that iterative solvers in scientific computing have self-convergence characteristics, allowing intermediate steps to use mixed precision. Refloat found a controllable balance between precision error and convergence speed. This was a cognitive breakthrough rather than just an engineering improvement.
Impact: It expanded the scope of ReRAM acceleration from graph/neural networks to scientific computing, opening up new research areas for HPC+ReRAM. With 11 citations, it showed a solid performance in SC papers.
3. TAPA: A Scalable Task-Parallel Dataflow Programming Framework for Modern FPGAs with Co-Optimization of HLS and Physical Design (2022) Description: Proposed a end-to-end compilation framework called TAPA, which allows users to describe computations using C++ task parallel dataflow programs. It automatically compiles into high-frequency FPGA accelerators. The key innovation was the simultaneous optimization of high-level synthesis (HLS) and physical layout, solving the problem of frequency collapse in large-scale FPGA designs.
Why it couldn’t be done before: Mainstream tools like Xilinx Vitis HLS considered HLS and physical implementation as separate stages, resulting in a significant decrease in frequency of logic generated by HLS after layout and routing. The key insight was that task parallel granularity naturally aligned with the SLR (Super Logic Region) physical partitions of FPGA. By recognizing physical constraints during the HLS stage, high productivity and performance could be achieved simultaneously. This required deep understanding of both FPGA architecture and HLS internal mechanisms, reflecting cross-layer abstraction capabilities.
Impact: It became an important basic construction tool for FPGA accelerator research in academia. Later, TAPA-CS was further extended to distributed multi-FPGA scenarios. It reduced the development threshold for high-performance FPGA accelerators.
4. NDSEARCH: Accelerating Graph-Traversal-Based Approximate Nearest Neighbor Search through Near Data Processing (2023) Description: Proposed a near-data processing acceleration scheme for approximate nearest neighbor search based on graph traversal in vector databases, such as HNSW/NSG algorithms. This work was published in the computer architecture conference ISCA, directly targeting the underlying indexing core of LLM retrieval-enhanced generation (RAG).
Why it couldn’t be done before: Graph traversal algorithms for ANNS were mainly regarded as software/algorithms problems before 2022. It was only during 2022-2023 that the explosive growth of LLM+RAG made ANNS a critical load in data centers, giving rise to strong motivation for hardware acceleration. In terms of computing power, there was a lack of sufficient empirical data to prove that ANNS was a memory bandwidth bottleneck rather than a computational bottleneck. NDSEARCH provided key evidence through profiling, making the near-data processing scheme persuasive.
Impact: With 22 citations, it shows to be one of the most cited works so far. It happened to coincide with the time when LLM infrastructure research was in full swing. It directed the attention of the architecture community towards the vector database track, driving a large number of ANNS accelerator studies later on.
Research Directions Near-memory/in-memory computing and hardware accelerator design (ReRAM, HBM, NDP) Sparse linear algebra and graph computing acceleration (SpMV, SpMM, graph traversal) FPGA high-level synthesis and programming frameworks (TAPA, RapidStream) System acceleration for AI/LLM infrastructure (ANNS, vector databases, RAG) Methodology Evolution In the early stage (2017-2019), simulation-based computing paradigms were used. ReRAM physical properties were utilized to perform matrix operations. The core methodology was the analysis of the trade-off between “precision and energy efficiency”. From 2020 to 2021, the methodology shifted towards sparse accelerator design in the digital domain. The methodology was upgraded to “dataflow analysis + memory access pattern modeling”, focusing on solving problems such as load imbalance and random memory access in sparse computing. The release of Pyxis dataset also reflected the emphasis on system evaluation methodologies. In 2022, the methodology of “cross-layer collaborative optimization” was introduced. TAPA simultaneously optimized three levels: programming model, HLS compiler, and physical design. This marked a shift from single accelerator to a complete toolchain. In 2023, the methodology further evolved towards “workload-driven architecture design”. NDSEARCH started by analyzing performance bottlenecks based on real LLM application scenarios, then derived hardware architecture decisions, reflecting a mature system research paradigm centered around application requirements.
Overall, the evolution path of methodology is: simulation-based near-memory computing → digital sparse acceleration → compiler and hardware collaboration → AI-application-driven architecture. The continuous focus was on memory bandwidth bottlenecks, with solutions evolving from simulation computing to HBM utilization and then to near-data processing.
Domain Impact Linghao Song’s contributions in the field of computer architecture are reflected in two dimensions: one is directional contributions. GraphR (2017 Best Paper) and NDSEARCH (ISCA 2023) sent guiding research signals at two key moments: ReRAM graph acceleration and vector database acceleration. The former inspired a generation of ReRAM accelerator work, while the latter coincided with the LLM infrastructure boom, leading to widespread follow-up efforts. The second dimension is infrastructure contributions. The TAPA framework was used as an open-source tool, reducing the entry barrier for FPGA accelerator research. This had a multiplier effect. From the citation distribution, NDSEARCH’s 22 citations were concentrated within just one year after its publication in 2023, indicating its strong short-term influence in the intersection of AI systems and architecture. This is highly consistent with current LLM infrastructure research hotspots.

summary: Linghao Song is a researcher specializing in computer architecture. His research focuses on memory-based computing as the core theme throughout his work. In 2017, he won the Best Paper Award for his work on ReRAM-based graph processing accelerators. This achievement laid the foundation for his future contributions in this field.
title: Linghao Song — Researcher Profile

content:
Nathan Inkawhich — Research Analysis Report Analysis Mode: fast | Analysis Date: 2026-03-16T00:44:17
Rating: Rising Star (55.8/100) Basic Metrics Metric Value Institution Unknown h-index 14 Total Citations 1,026 Citations in Last 5 Years 442 Number of Papers 39 Papers Published in Top Journals 12 Publication Period 2017 - 2025 Semantic Scholar 52121635 Research Track Nathan Inkawhich is a researcher focusing on the intersection of adversarial machine learning and SAR (Synthetic Aperture Radar) remote sensing recognition. Since 2017, he has been developing in two parallel directions: defense/remote sensing applications and deep learning security. His academic breakthrough occurred between 2019-2020, when his papers in CVPR, NeurIPS (two papers each), and ICLR established him as a leader in the field of transferable adversarial attacks. Four papers published in top journals received over 500 citations, marking a crucial period in his academic career.
Since 2021, he has made a systematic shift in his research focus: from “how to create stronger attacks” to “how to build more reliable recognition systems”. His work focuses on open-world SAR-ATR (Automatic Target Recognition), out-of-distribution (OOD) detection, and multi-modal remote sensing image understanding. This has led to the development of a core approach for “safe and reliable AI recognition systems”. Since 2023, he has been systematically evaluating the applicability and vulnerabilities of basic models (such as CLIP, DINOv2) in adversarial attacks and SAR recognition. His research scope has expanded from attack-defense mechanisms to practical evaluation of basic models.
Breakthrough Works 1. Feature Space Perturbations Yield More Transferable Adversarial Examples (2019) Description: Proposed a method for black-box transferable adversarial attacks based on deep feature space perturbations. Unlike previous mainstream methods that applied perturbations to the output layer (softmax/logit layers) to directly manipulate classification results, this work applies perturbations to intermediate feature layers. This significantly improves the success rate of target-oriented adversarial samples in black-box scenarios and provides interpretable analysis of the model’s internal representation.
Why it couldn’t be done before: Previous research generally believed that adversarial perturbations should be applied to the output layer (softmax/logit layers) to directly control classification results. There was a lack of systematic understanding of the semantic structure of intermediate feature layers. The key insight was that researchers didn’t realize that the class separability structure of feature layers was the fundamental source of transferability, rather than the decision boundary of the output layer. This work systematically linked feature distribution modeling with the mechanism of transferability for the first time.
Impact: Covered 199 citations and became an important reference baseline for subsequent research on transferable adversarial attacks. It promoted a shift from the “output layer attack” paradigm to the “feature layer attack” paradigm, and inspired a series of subsequent studies based on intermediate layer representations in attack-defense scenarios.
2. Transferable Perturbations of Deep Feature Distributions (2020) Description: By modeling the class-level distribution (mean and covariance) of deep features, a method for adversarial attacks was designed that completely separated from output layer information. This achieved the best performance among target-oriented black-box transfer attacks at that time.
Why it couldn’t be done before: Previous feature layer attack methods usually only targeted individual feature activation points, rather than statistically modeling the overall feature distribution. Accurate estimation of class-condition feature distributions required a large number of similar samples and corresponding distribution modeling techniques. This idea was not introduced into adversarial attack design at that time. Computing power wasn’t the bottleneck; the key was introducing the statistical perspective of “feature distribution”.
Impact: Covered 98 citations (published in ICLR). It introduced statistical distribution modeling into adversarial attack design, providing a methodological basis for subsequent generative adversarial samples and feature-matching-based attack methods.
3. Perturbing Across the Feature Hierarchy to Improve Standard and Strict Blackbox Attack Transferability (2020) Description: Proposed a method for cross-layer feature hierarchy perturbations (PAH). By applying perturbations to multiple feature layers and weighting them together, this method improved the transferability of standard black-box attacks. It also maintained effectiveness under stricter black-box constraints (where the source model and target model had no overlapping labeled spaces).
Why it couldn’t be done before: Previous work usually only targeted a single feature layer for attacks, lacking an understanding that “different levels of features contain complementary adversarial information”. Multi-layer joint optimization faced engineering challenges in designing optimization objectives and gradient balancing. This work solved the problem of layer-weighting distribution through systematic ablation experiments.
Impact: Covered 100 citations (NeurIPS 2020). It complemented the ICLR paper from the same year in terms of methodology, forming a complete theoretical system for Inkawhich’s research on feature-layer adversarial attacks.
4. DVERGE: Diversifying Vulnerabilities for Enhanced Robust Generation of Ensembles (2020) Description: Identified overlapping adversarial vulnerabilities in CNN integrated models (different models failed to work with the same input). The DVERGE method enhances the overall robustness of integration by actively diversifying the adversarial vulnerabilities of each sub-model, making it difficult for attacks targeting a single sub-model to be transferred to the entire integration.
Why it couldn’t be done before: Integrated learning has long been seen as a natural solution for adversarial robustness. However, the core problem of “vulnerability overlap” had not been systematically quantified and studied before. It was necessary to establish a theoretical understanding of transferable adversarial attacks (which is exactly Inkawhich’s previous work) before using this mechanism to design diversified training objectives.
Impact: Covered 128 citations (NeurIPS 2020). It transformed the theoretical understanding of adversarial transferability into defensive design principles, opening up research directions for integrated robustness based on “vulnerability diversification”.
5. Bridging a Gap in SAR-ATR: Training on Fully Synthetic and Testing on Measured Data (2021) Description: Systematically studied the domain adaptation problem between fully synthetic SAR data training and real measurement data testing (Sim-to-Real gap). Proposed a transfer learning framework for SAR-ATR, proving that synthetic data could effectively support model training in defense scenarios where labeled real data was extremely scarce.
Why it couldn’t be done before: The computational cost of generating synthetic SAR data (electromagnetic scattering simulation) dropped significantly in the late 2010s. At the same time, deep domain adaptation methods became more mature. The combination of both made the Sim-to-Real approach feasible for SAR-ATR. Previous problems were a double bottleneck: insufficient data (very few real SAR labeled samples) and low simulation accuracy.
Impact: Covered 97 citations. It provided a practical path for the defense/remote sensing community to train SAR-ATR models with low data costs, making it a benchmark work widely cited in this field.
Research Directions Transferable black-box adversarial attacks (based on feature layer distribution modeling) SAR automatic target recognition (SAR-ATR) and open-world robustness Out-of-distribution detection (OOD) and fine-grained recognition Multi-modal remote sensing image understanding (SAR + EO fusion) Evaluation of basic models’ applicability in adversarial scenarios and SAR domains Methodological Evolution Inkawhich’s methodological evolution went through three clear stages. The first stage (2018-2020): Focused on “attack” as the core, starting from pixel-level adversarial perturbations and gradually moving to feature layers. First, single-layer feature mean perturbation was used (CVPR 2019), then class feature distribution modeling was introduced (ICLR 2020), and finally cross-layer feature hierarchy perturbations were developed (NeurIPS 2020). This formed a complete theoretical system for feature space adversarial attacks. DVERGE reversed the attack mechanism into a defensive tool, completing an attack-defense loop.
The second stage (2021-2023): The methodology shifted from “creating adversarial samples” to “building reliable recognition systems”. In the field of SAR-ATR, Sim-to-Real transfer learning and few-shot learning were introduced. In OOD detection, data augmentation strategies like Mixup Outlier Exposure were used, and fine-grained and hierarchical OOD inference was explored. The method design paid more attention to practical scenario constraints (data scarcity, open-world scenarios, deployment environment differences). The third stage (2023-to-date): Shifted to a systematic evaluation perspective, conducting comprehensive benchmark tests on the adversarial robustness and SAR applicability of basic models like CLIP/DINOv2. The methodology evolved from “proposing new methods” to “establishing evaluation frameworks and understanding boundaries”.
Domain Impact Inkawhich’s core contributions to the field were concentrated at two intersections: one was introducing statistical feature distribution modeling into transferable adversarial attack design, which promoted a deeper understanding of the mechanism behind “why adversarial samples can be transferred across models”. His four papers published in top journals between 2019-2020 received over 520 citations, making it an important reference document for this sub-field. The other contribution was systematic work on the practical application of SAR intelligent recognition, covering core challenges like Sim-to-Real transfer learning, few-shot learning, open-world OOD detection, and multi-modal fusion. This had significant practical value for the defense/remote sensing AI community. Overall, he is a leading researcher with distinct domain characteristics: he established a unique research identity in the intersection of adversarial ML and remote sensing recognition. However, his influence was mainly concentrated in specific sub-fields, and he hasn’t achieved widespread influence that would establish himself as a foundational figure in the overall field.

summary: Nathan Inkawhich is a researcher who specializes in tackling the intersection of machine learning and SAR (Synthetic Aperture Radar) remote sensing recognition technologies. Since 2017, he has been working on both defense/remote sensing applications and deep learning security. His academic career reached its peak during 2019-2020, when he presented papers at CVPR and NeurIPS.

Wu Lin — Researcher Analysis Report Analysis Mode: fast | Analysis Time: 2026-03-16T00:19:55
Rating: New Star (52.2/100) Basic Metrics Metric Value Institution Unknown h-index 26 Total Citations 2,130 Recent Citations 125 Number of Papers 56 Papers in Top Journals 0 Publication Years 2001 - 2025 Semantic Scholar 8531953 Research Track It should be noted that there is a serious issue of confusion regarding the names of researchers in this paper list. “Wu Lin” is a common Chinese name, and the database aggregates the achievements of at least 5 researchers with similar names from different fields: ① Plant Soil Ecologist (Continuous cropping obstacles/rhizosphere microbiome, 2001-2025, forming the core main line); ② Computer Vision Researcher (Pedestrian recognition, 2018+); ③ Quantum Photonic Physicist (2018+); ④ Machine Learning Optimization Researcher (Natural gradients, KFAC, 2021+); ⑤ Radio Astronomer (2019+). The following analysis focuses on the main researcher with a complete academic context, concentrated citation volume, and coherent theme—Wu Lin in the field of plant rhizosphere microecology.
The core academic contribution of this researcher lies in systematically revealing the rhizosphere microecological mechanisms of continuous cropping obstacles in Chinese herbal medicines. His academic career has gone through three clear stages: The first stage (2001-2011) started from traditional agriculture, where he was the first to introduce metaproteomics into rhizospheric soil research. Using Rehmannia glutinosa as a model system, he established a technical platform for studying continuous cropping obstacles. The second stage (2013-2018) focused on mechanism analysis. Through chemical analysis of root exudates using HPLC, high-throughput sequencing (pyrosequencing, Illumina), and metagenomics, he constructed a causal chain of “phenolic acid accumulation → pathogenic microorganism proliferation → rhizosphere microbial community imbalance → continuous cropping reduction in yield.” The third stage (2018-2022) shifted to intervention strategies, exploring the effects of bioorganic fertilizers and intercropping systems on continuous cropping obstacles. This marked a transition from mechanism research to application-oriented studies.
Breakthrough Works 1. Characterization of metaproteomics in crop rhizospheric soil (2011) Description: For the first time, metaproteomics technology was systematically applied to crop rhizospheric soil research. Functional microbial proteins were directly identified in rhizospheric soil, revealing the actual metabolic activities of rhizospheric microbial communities, rather than relying solely on the “potential presence” information of 16S rRNA genes.
Why it couldn’t be done before: Technical bottleneck: The rhizospheric soil matrix was complex, and humic acids caused serious interference. Protein extraction and mass spectrometry identification were not yet mature before 2011. Additionally, the database had insufficient coverage of soil microbial protein sequences, limiting the depth of identification. The maturity of high-throughput mass spectrometry and bioinformatics processes made this breakthrough possible.
Impact: It pioneered the research direction of metaproteomics in Chinese agricultural soils. The paper was cited 130 times and provided a core methodological framework for subsequent studies on continuous cropping obstacles. It promoted the transformation of the field from community structure description to functional analysis.
2. Plant-microbe rhizosphere interactions mediated by Rehmannia glutinosa root exudates under consecutive monoculture (2015) Description: Through four years of field experiments combined with laboratory verification, it was demonstrated that phenolic substances secreted by the roots of Rehmannia glutinosa had a cumulative effect under sterile conditions. This directly led to an imbalance in the rhizospheric microbial community structure, and key pathogenic secondary metabolites were identified. A “root exudate-microorganism-plant health” triple interaction model was established.
Why it couldn’t be done before: Lack of key insights: Previous studies attributed continuous cropping obstacles to soil nutrient depletion or single pathogens, lacking a systematic understanding of plant-microbial chemical interactions. The long-term experimental accumulation required for four years of field trials and the popularization of HPLC-mass spectrometry technology were prerequisites.
Impact: The paper was cited 135 times and became a landmark paper in the research field of continuous cropping obstacles. It provided a complete biochemical evidence chain for continuous cropping obstacles in Rehmannia glutinosa, promoting sustainable cultivation of Chinese medicinal materials as an independent research direction.
3. The role of organic acids on microbial deterioration in the Radix pseudostellariae rhizosphere under continuous monoculture regimes (2017) Description: Using Radix pseudostellariae as the subject, through three years of field trials combined with transcriptome sequencing, it was explained at the molecular level how organic acids (oxalic acid, succinic acid, etc.) selectively promoted the proliferation of pathogenic fungi (Talaromyces helicus, Kosakonia sacchari), revealing the targeted microbial regulation mechanism of phenolic substances.
Why it couldn’t be done before: The cost of transcriptome sequencing decreased significantly after 2015, making it possible to analyze gene expression responses to organic acids. The results of pathogen identification from previous studies (Frontiers in Microbiology, 2016) provided a clear research target for this study.
Impact: The paper was cited 94 times and advanced the research on continuous cropping obstacles to the gene regulation level, providing a theoretical basis for targeted biocontrol strategies (such as selecting antagonistic microorganisms that can degrade specific organic acids).
4. Belowground Interactions Impact the Soil Bacterial Community, Soil Fertility, and Crop Yield in Maize/Peanut Intercropping Systems (2018) Description: Using a physical isolation experimental design (non-isolation/semi-isolation with nylon net/complete isolation with plastic film), it quantified the independent contributions of root contact, root exudate exchange, and competitive nutrient absorption to the rhizosphere community in maize/peanut intercropping systems. Different physical pathways of rhizosphere interaction were isolated for the first time.
Why it couldn’t be done before: Experimental design innovation: Previous intercropping studies could not distinguish between different physical mechanisms of root interactions. This three-treatment isolation design was a key methodological breakthrough. Combined with the reduced cost of 16S amplicon sequencing, large-scale sample analysis became possible.
Impact: The paper was cited 106 times and provided a mechanism basis for optimizing intercropping systems. It promoted the development of the concept of “designed rhizosphere microecology” and had direct guiding value for sustainable agricultural practices.
5. Antagonistic Activity of Trichoderma spp. Against Fusarium oxysporum in Rhizosphere of Radix pseudostellariae Triggers the Expression of Host Defense Genes and Improves Its Growth Under Long-Term Monoculture System (2021) Description: It was proven that Trichoderma spp. not only directly antagonistically inhibits Fusarium oxysporum but also activates the expression of host defense genes in Rehmannia glutinosa, achieving an “indirect immune activation” effect. This marked a transition from passive mechanism analysis to active intervention verification.
Why it couldn’t be done before: It required integrating interdisciplinary capabilities between macro-agricultural experiments and molecular plant pathology analysis. Effective biocontrol experiments could only be designed after identifying the pathogen (Fusarium oxysporum, 2015, 2017).
Impact: The paper was cited 53 times and provided direct application evidence for biological control strategies of continuous cropping obstacles. It marked a new stage in the researcher’s transformation from mechanism research to green agricultural solutions.
Research Directions rhizosphere biochemistry mechanisms of continuous cropping obstacles in Chinese herbal medicines (phenolic acids/organic acids-microorganisms-plant interactions) Diversity and function analysis of rhizospheric microbiomes (metaproteomics, metagenomics, high-throughput sequencing) Restoration strategies for rhizosphere microecology through intercropping systems and bioorganic fertilizers Relationships between soil microbial communities and vegetation types/forest succession Methodological Evolution The researcher’s methodological evolution showed clear technical-driven characteristics. In the early stage (2001-2011), traditional microbial culture techniques and two-dimensional electrophoresis were used. In 2011, the introduction of metaproteomics was a first technological leap, marking a shift from “culturing-dependent” to “culturing-independent” research paradigms. From 2013 to 2016, with the popularization of pyrosequencing, researchers widely used 16S rRNA gene amplicon sequencing combined with CLPP (community level physiological maps) to describe community structures. At the same time, HPLC was introduced for quantitative analysis of root exudates, enabling correlation analysis between “microbial community changes” and “chemical driving factors.” Since 2017, the introduction of transcriptome sequencing enabled research to go deeper into gene regulation, revealing the molecular mechanisms of microorganisms in response to chemical signals. From 2018 to 2022, the methodology became more integrated: metagenomics (functional potential prediction) was combined with field agricultural indicators (yield, enzyme activity, soil nutrients), forming a “mechanism-effect” dual-validation system. Throughout the process, researchers always adhered to the dual-track model of “field verification + laboratory mechanism analysis,” avoiding the problem of disconnection between pure genomics research and actual agriculture.
Domain Impact Wu Lin made foundational contributions to the field of rhizosphere ecology of continuous cropping obstacles in Chinese herbal medicines. His impact is reflected at two levels: Methodological level, where he was the first to introduce modern genomics technologies such as metaproteomics and metagenomics into the study of Chinese medicinal materials’ rhizospheres, providing a technical demonstration for the entire field. At the mechanism level, the causal chain of “root exudates (phenolic acids/organic acids) → rhizosphere microbial imbalance → continuous cropping reduction in yield” has become a consensus in the field and has been cited and extended by many subsequent studies. Considering that his h-index is 26 and he has received 2,130 citations, but only 125 of them were from the past five years, his academic influence peaked between 2011 and 2018. His core contributions have been fully absorbed by the field. His research directly serves the issue of sustainable cultivation of traditional Chinese medicinal materials, having clear industrial application directions beyond academic value. It has promoted the application of bioorganic fertilizers and intercropping systems in the production of Chinese medicinal materials. Note: The Semantic Scholar database has mixed the achievements of several researchers with similar names in this file (computer vision, quantum physics, radio astronomy, etc.). These papers have been excluded from this analysis as they do not belong to the same researcher.

Xiaoxiao Liu — Research Analyst Report Analysis Mode: fast | Analysis Time: 2026-03-16T00:47:49
Rating: Active Researcher (40.2/100) Basic Metrics Metric Value Institution Unknown h-index 12 Total Citations 451 Recent Citations 174 Number of Papers 23 Papers in Top Journals 0 Publication Years 2005 - 2023 Semantic Scholar 46522031 Research Trajectory It should be noted that there is an obvious name confusion issue in this paper list. The papers cover completely unrelated fields such as cancer biology, GPU architecture, plant immunity, hyperspectral image processing, genomics, and LLM prompt engineering. They obviously come from multiple researchers with the same name. Based on publication years, citation numbers, and thematic consistency, at least three different Xiaoxiao Liu can be identified: (1) Molecular Oncology方向 (contributing to most of the 451 citations, including 62 citations in the highest-cited miR-1271 pancreatic cancer paper and papers of the level of Nature Communications/JEM/PNAS); (2) Computer Architecture/Image Processing direction (GPU memory design, hyperspectral denoising); (3) LLM/AI security direction (prompt engineering, security evaluation, etc. from 2023 - 2025). The following analysis mainly focuses on the Molecular Oncology researcher who is the most representative and has the most complete literature records. His research trajectory shows a gradual progression from microRNA regulation to p53-MDM2 targeted therapy, then to E3 ubiquitin ligase tumor suppression, and finally to CHIP cloning hematopoiesis.
Breakthrough Works 1. miR-1271 inhibits migration, invasion, and epithelial-mesenchymal transition by targeting ZEB1 and TWIST1 in pancreatic cancer cells (2016) Description: For the first time, it was systematically demonstrated that miR-1271 inhibits the migration, invasion, and epithelial-mesenchymal transition of pancreatic cancer cells by targeting both ZEB1 and TWIST1, two core EMT transcription factors. This provides a dual-target miRNA regulation framework for the metastasis mechanism of pancreatic cancer.
Why it couldn’t be done before: Before 2016, the cost of high-throughput sequencing for miRNAs was still high, and systematic studies on miRNA regulatory networks in pancreatic cancer were limited. The mechanism by which ZEB1 and TWIST1 were co-targeted by the same miRNA had not been revealed. It was only with the popularization of NGS technology and the maturity of bioinformatics analysis tools that this dual-target regulation axis could be systematically screened and verified.
Impact: This paper became the most cited paper of this researcher, with 62 citations. It promoted the establishment of a multi-target miRNA regulation strategy in pancreatic cancer metastasis research and has important reference value for subsequent studies on EMI-related therapeutic targets.
2. Antitumor immunity enhances the therapeutic effects of p53 activation on acute myeloid leukemia (2019) Description: Published in Nature Communications, it demonstrated that the orally active p53-MDM2 interaction inhibitor DS-5272 not only exerts anti-tumor effects by directly activating the p53 signal in AML cells, but also synergistically enhances the host’s anti-tumor immune response. This revealed the synergistic mechanism between targeted therapy and tumor immunity.
Why it couldn’t be done before: p53-MDM2 inhibitors were long considered to act through cell-autonomous pathways. The long-term separation between immunology research and targeted drug research made it difficult to discover this synergy relationship. Around 2019, the integration of immun oncology and small molecule targeted therapy became a research hotspot. The maturity of single-cell immunotyping technologies such as CyTOF (published with supporting datasets in the same year) made it possible to accurately quantify the dynamic changes of immune cell subsets.
Impact: Published in Nature Communications with 42 citations, this paper expanded the p53-targeted therapy research for AML from a single-cell killing paradigm to an immune regulation dimension. It provided a theoretical basis for the design of clinical protocols using combined immun checkpoint inhibitors.
3. Mutational inactivation of mTORC1 repressor gene DEPDC5 in human gastrointestinal stromal tumors (2019) Description: Published in PNAS, it reported for the first time that the mutant inactivation of the mTORC1 negative regulator gene DEPDC5 is an important driver of GIST progression (rather than initiation). This clarified a new molecular mechanism for the malignant progression of GIST after KIT/PDGFRA mutations.
Why it couldn’t be done before: DEPDC5 was previously mainly studied in neurological diseases such as focal epilepsy, and its association with GIST had not been discovered for a long time. Research on the progression mechanism of GIST requires large-scale genomic sequencing data. The large-scale application of next-generation sequencing in clinical tumor samples, combined with systematic research designs on the evolutionary trajectory of ‘micro-GIST→progressive GIST’, is a prerequisite for this discovery.
Impact: With 34 citations, it provided a new explanation for the progression mechanism after GIST targeted therapy resistance. The mTOR pathway thus entered the list of potential GIST treatment targets, with direct clinical translation value.
4. E3 ligase MKRN3 is a tumor suppressor regulating PABPC1 ubiquitination in non-small cell lung cancer (2021) Description: Published in Journal of Experimental Medicine, it was found that the central precocious puberty-causing gene MKRN3 is a true tumor suppressor in NSCLC. It regulates global protein synthesis by targeting PABPC1 ubiquitination modification, revealing a new tumor suppression axis of ‘E3 ligase-mRNA-binding protein-proteome homeostasis’.
Why it couldn’t be done before: MKRN3 was previously only recognized as a gene regulating puberty development. Its function in tumors was completely unknown. This discovery required a unique perspective of cross-correlating clinical central precocious puberty genetics with cancer genomic databases, as well as mature ubiquitomics protein profiling techniques to identify PABPC1 as a substrate. This cognitive leap from ’endocrine disease gene→tumor suppressor’ depends on the improvement of large-scale cancer genome atlas databases such as TCGA.
Impact: With 41 citations, it provided a new tumor suppression mechanism and potential treatment targets for NSCLC. It also pioneered a new paradigm of studying the functional reutilization of developmental genes in tumors.
5. CHIP-associated mutant ASXL1 in blood cells promotes solid tumor progression (2022) Description: It revealed that ASXL1 mutants related to cloning hematopoiesis (CHIP) can promote the progression of solid tumors. This established a causal relationship between cloned mutations in the blood system and tumors in non-hematopoietic systems, providing important evidence for CHIP’s cross-system impact on tumors.
Why it couldn’t be done before: The recognition of CHIP as an independent risk factor only emerged with large-scale population cohort studies from 2014 - 2017. To associate CHIP with solid tumor progression, precise CHIP mouse models, solid tumor transplantation systems, and a deep understanding of the functions of bone marrow-derived cells in the tumor microenvironment were required. These technologies and cognitive conditions matured around 2022.
Impact: With 30 citations, it extended CHIP research from cardiovascular diseases and blood malignancies to the field of solid tumors. It has important clinical significance for risk stratification and treatment strategy formulation for elderly cancer patients.
Research Directions Synergistic mechanisms between targeted therapy and tumor immunity in AML/MDS (p53-MDM2, IMPDH inhibition) Study on the functions of E3 ubiquitin ligases in tumors (STUB1/RUNX1, MKRN3/PABPC1) Molecular drivers and new targets discovery for solid tumors (GIST, NSCLC, pancreatic cancer) Role of microRNAs in cancer metastasis and EMT regulation Cross-system impact of cloning hematopoiesis (CHIP) and tumor progression Methodological Evolution In the early stage (2005 - 2016), research focused mainly on single molecular mechanisms. The classical cell line experiments + overexpression/knockout verification paradigm was used to study miRNA target gene regulation. From 2017 - 2019, a more integrated research strategy was adopted, introducing mouse in vivo models, drug intervention experiments, and single-cell immunotyping (CyTOF). This achieved a transition from ‘mechanism discovery’ to ’therapeutic validation’. A representative work is the AML research published in Nature Communications. From 2020 - 2022, it was further upgraded to a two-layer strategy of systematic screening + mechanism deep exploration: using CRISPR functional genomics for target screening or ubiquitomics mass spectrometry for target discovery (such as CDK1 screening, MKRN3-PABPC1 axis), followed by verification of clinical relevance with patient sample databases. The methodological maturity has been significantly improved.
Domain Impact The core contributions of this researcher in the field of molecular oncology are concentrated in three aspects: (1) It promoted the paradigm shift in AML targeted therapy from simple cell killing to a ’targeted + immune synergy’ model; (2) It systematically expanded the understanding of E3 ubiquitin ligases as tumor suppressor factors. In particular, the discovery of MKRN3 effectively connected developmental biology with oncology; (3) It provided new molecular targets and treatment strategies for refractory tumors such as GIST and NSCLC. Overall, this researcher is a stable contributor to the intersection of blood oncology and solid tumors. His publications are in high-level journals such as JEM, Nature Communications, PNAS, EMBO Molecular Medicine. However, it should be noted that his h-index of 12 is relatively low compared to the number of papers and top journals. This may be due to data distortion caused by aggregation of multiple researchers. The actual academic influence of a single researcher may be underestimated.

content:
Xiongfei Yu — Research Analyst Report Analysis Mode: fast | Analysis Time: 2026-03-16T00:07:51
Rating: Early Researcher (28.3/100) Basic Metrics Metric Value Institution Unknown h-index 6 Total Citations 248 Recent 5 Years of Citations 0 Number of Papers 37 Papers Published in Top Journals 0 Publication Period 1996 - 2019 Semantic Scholar 2112440444 Research Trajectory Xiongfei Yu’s academic trajectory shows a rare cross-disciplinary multi-stage development pattern, likely resulting from the combined efforts of multiple researchers with the same name. The first phase (1996—2004) focused on the field of petroleum engineering, specializing in the prediction of gas condensate production, CO₂ injection for enhanced oil recovery (EOR), and the evaluation of gas injection reservoirs. This work had a clear engineering background from Chinese oilfields such as Liaohe and Daqing. The second phase (2005—2011) shifted the research focus to geotechnical and civil engineering, with contributions including finite element analysis of dam stability, flood protection dams in Louisiana, USA, and pile foundation testing in Georgia. These works had North American engineering characteristics and had little connection to petroleum engineering. The third phase (2017—2019) returned to petroleum engineering, but the research level was elevated to rock physics and unconventional reservoir mechanisms, focusing on the compaction of unconsolidated sandstone, the impact of sand production on fluid flow, and spontaneous imbibition in tight sandstone, reflecting the development of shale oil/tight oil in China. The most cited paper (2019, 108 citations) represents the peak of his academic influence.
Breakthrough Works 1. New insights into spontaneous imbibition in tight oil sandstones with NMR (2019) Description: Using nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) technology, this study systematically revealed the microscopic mechanisms and quantitative laws of spontaneous imbibition in tight sandstone reservoirs. This provides an experimental basis for understanding the mechanism of crude oil production during the post-fracturing period of well layering, and directly guides the optimization of fracturing fluid discharge and well layering systems.
Why it couldn’t be done before: Tight sandstone has extremely small pore sizes (nanometer scale), making it impossible to characterize the distribution and migration of fluids in pores in situ and dynamically using traditional mercury injection and core experiments. The maturity of NMR core analysis technology and the widespread use of high-field instruments provide a prerequisite for such research. Additionally, the large-scale commercial development of shale oil/tight oil in China (since 2015) has created urgent engineering needs, which have driven the availability of experimental funds and sample sources.
Impact: This paper was cited 108 times and became an important reference for studying spontaneous imbibition in tight reservoirs. It has contributed to the rapid development of the engineering direction of “well layering optimization—capillary force oil production” in China’s unconventional oil and gas industry.
2. Stability analyses of dam abutments by 3D elasto-plastic finite-element method: A case study of Houhe gravity-arch dam in China (2005) Description: Using a three-dimensional elastic-plastic finite element method, this study systematically analyzed the stability of dam shoulders in China’s Houhe gravity arch dam. It provided a numerical simulation framework that can be used for practical engineering verification. This work was cited 39 times and represents the most influential achievement of this phase.
Why it couldn’t be done before: The use of three-dimensional elastic-plastic finite element models requires high computational resources, and three-dimensional rock body simulations were not widely used in China before 2005. Additionally, large-scale water conservancy projects in China (such as the Three Gorges Project) provided case studies and engineering requirements for this research.
Impact: This work provides a methodological framework for analyzing the seismic resistance and dam shoulder stability of gravity dams in China. It has been continuously cited in literature on civil engineering and geotechnical engineering.
3. Fluid flow with compaction and sand production in unconsolidated sandstone reservoir (2018) Description: Through single-phase displacement experiments, this study quantitatively measured the combined effects of compaction and sand production on the seepage behavior of unconsolidated sandstone reservoirs. It established a fluid flow model that takes into account reservoir damage, providing an experimental basis for predicting oil well productivity decline.
Why it couldn’t be done before: It was difficult to implement specialized high-pressure core clamping devices to simultaneously control compaction and sand production. The technical difficulty of experimental operations (sample preparation and encapsulation) for unconsolidated sandstone was relatively high, and previous experimental data were scarce.
Impact: This work was cited 23 times and provided an experimental basis for evaluating productivity and sand prevention designs in heavy oil and loose sandstone reservoirs (such as Liaohe Oilfield).
4. The variation mechanism of petrophysical properties and the effect of compaction on the relative permeability of an unconsolidated sandstone reservoir (2017) Description: This study systematically investigated the changes in petrophysical parameters (porosity, permeability) of unconsolidated sandstone reservoirs with compaction. It quantified the impact of compaction on the gas-water relative permeability curve, revealing the mechanism of productivity decline during production.
Why it couldn’t be done before: Relative permeability experiments were usually conducted on consolidated cores. The combination of dynamic compaction and two-phase seepage experiments in unconsolidated sandstone was technically challenging, and such data had been scarce for a long time.
Impact: This work was cited 18 times and filled the gap in research on the coupling between compaction and seepage in unconsolidated sandstone. It has practical reference value for the design of development plans for offshore heavy oil and loose sandstone reservoirs.
5. A SCREENING CANDIDATE RESERVOIR FOR GAS INJECTION WAY BASED ON CHARACTERISTIC PARAMETER’S COMPREHENSIVE WEIGHT OPTIMIZATION METHOD (2004) Description: This study proposed a method for screening candidate reservoirs for gas injection based on comprehensive weight optimization of characteristic parameters. It provided a quantitative decision-making framework for selecting reservoirs for CO₂/N₂/gas mixed phase flooding. This was a systematic contribution to the early EOR engineering practices.
Why it couldn’t be done before: Early reservoir screening often relied on empirical judgment of single parameters, lacking a systematic approach to multi-parameter optimization. At the same time, large-scale experiments with CO₂-EOR in China (early 2000s) provided data for refining screening criteria.
Impact: This work was cited 6 times and provided a reusable systematical screening approach for evaluating projects involving gas injection to improve oil recovery in Chinese oilfields.
Research Directions Rock physics of tight and unconsolidated sandstone reservoirs (compaction, sand production, relative permeability, spontaneous imbibition) Enhanced oil recovery through gas injection (CO₂/N₂/gas mixed phase flooding, reservoir screening evaluation) Geotechnical and civil engineering (finite element analysis of dam shoulder stability, geophysical exploration, flood protection dams) Sealing mechanism of unconventional reservoirs and development dynamics prediction Methodological Evolution Early (1996—2004) research relied mainly on engineering experience methods, including fuzzy hierarchical analysis method (FAHP), parameter optimization screening, and semi-empirical prediction models. These were primarily used as decision-making tools in oilfield engineering practices, with little experimental verification. Mid-term (2005—2011) methodology shifted towards numerical simulation (three-dimensional elastic-plastic finite element) and geophysical field testing (CPTu, seismic waves, borehole geophysics), reflecting the trend toward precision in the field of geotechnical engineering. Later period (2017—2019) methodology completed the transition from macroscopic engineering to microscopic mechanisms: focusing on high-pressure core physical experiments (single-phase/two-phase displacement, compaction loading devices) combined with NMR pore-scale characterization, supplemented by numerical simulation and theoretical models. This represents the mainstream paradigm of contemporary reservoir physics research.
Domain Impact Overall, Xiongfei Yu’s (or his group of researchers) academic influence shows a “late-stage concentrated outbreak” pattern: early and mid-period papers had scattered citations (with a peak of 39 citations per paper), while three rock physics papers published between 2017—2019 contributed approximately 149 citations, accounting for more than 60% of the total 248 citations. Among these, the NMR spontaneous imbibition paper from 2019 (108 citations) became a highly cited reference in the field of tight sandstone seepage research. This pattern indicates that researchers found key research opportunities during the rise of unconventional reservoirs and made concrete and quantifiable academic contributions to the development of shale oil/tight oil technology in China. However, with an h-index of only 6 and abnormally high citations in the past 5 years, it suggests there may be issues with merging authors with the same name in the database. It is recommended to verify this by combining information on the researchers’ institutions.

It is highly likely that Semantic Scholar has combined dozens of researchers with the same name into one profile. From the paper list, it can be clearly seen that there is no connection between these fields, such as superconductive thin films in 1990, intelligent transportation in 2003, thermal acoustics refrigeration in 2007, schistosomiasis epidemiology in 2009, bamboo invasion ecology in 2013, thermal acoustic engines in 2014, shale gas geology in 2016, and topological quantum field theory in 2023. It is impossible for the same person to have contributed to these fields. This profile has an h-index of 10 and 375 total citations, corresponding to 101 papers. This does not match the development trajectory of a single researcher. The following analysis is a梳理 of the most cited research lines in this mixed profile, not the academic trajectory of a single scholar.
Breakthrough Works 1. Shale Gas Formation and Occurrence in China: An Overview of the Current Status and Future Potential (2016) Description: A systematic review of the geological formation, reservoir characteristics, and development potential of shale gas in China. It was cited 56 times and is the most cited single paper in this profile. This work is an important review in the fields of Chinese shale gas industry policies and resource assessment.
Why couldn’t it be done before: Before 2016, there was insufficient data on shale gas exploration in China. The systematic geological evaluation in regions like the Sichuan Basin was still in its early stages. At the same time, the shale gas revolution in the United States provided a technical framework for comparison studies.
Impact: It provided a systematic reference for evaluating shale gas resources in China and supported discussions on shale gas development paths at the energy policy level.
2. Plant species diversity and dynamics in forests invaded by Moso bamboo (Phyllostachys edulis) in Tianmu Mountain Nature Reserve (2013) Description: A study of the impact of bamboo expansion on forest plant diversity in the Tianmu Mountain Nature Reserve. It was cited 53 times and is an important empirical study in the field of biological invasion ecology regarding bamboo spread mechanisms.
Why couldn’t it be done before: Long-term monitoring data from fixed plots were needed to support this research. The long-term vegetation monitoring system in Tianmu Mountain became more complete only in the late 2000s. At the same time, the ecological threats posed by bamboo expansion did not attract systematic attention from the domestic ecology community until the early 2010s.
Impact: It provided ecological evidence for the protection of subtropical forests in China and the management policies regarding bamboo. It promoted research on the impact of bamboo expansion on biodiversity.
3. Heat transfer characteristics of oscillating flow regenerator filled with circular tubes or parallel plates (2007) Description: A study on the heat transfer characteristics of oscillating flow regenerators, which are core components of thermal acoustics systems. It was cited 35 times and is a basic experimental research in the fields of thermal acoustics refrigeration and cryogenic engineering.
Why couldn’t it be done before: The mechanism of oscillating flow heat transfer was complex, and early measuring instruments were not accurate enough to distinguish the heat transfer differences between circular tubes and parallel plates. By the early 2000s, the experimental platform for thermal acoustics systems became more mature, making precise parameterized research possible.
Impact: It provided an experimental basis for the optimization design of regenerators in thermal acoustics refrigeration machines and thermal engines. Subsequent papers on thermal engines from 2014-2015 (cited 27-30 times) were based on this work.
4. Study of a liquid-piston traveling-wave thermoacoustic heat engine with different working gases (2014) Description: A study on the performance of traveling-wave thermoacoustic engines using different working gases. It was cited 30 times and represents a systematic experimental exploration in the field of thermal acoustics energy conversion using liquid piston configurations.
Why couldn’t it be done before: The sealing and dynamic stability issues of liquid piston thermal acoustic systems were difficult to solve engineering-wise in the early days. The theory of traveling-wave thermoacoustics became more mature after Backhaus & Swift (1999), but experimental verification work only emerged in large quantities in the 2010s.
Impact: It promoted the transformation of thermal engines from laboratory concepts to engineering applications, especially in the potential application areas of low-grade waste heat recovery.
5. An Evaluating Method of Public Transit Accessibility for Urban Areas Based on GIS (2016) Description: A method for evaluating public transit accessibility in urban areas based on GIS. It was cited 21 times and integrates passenger behavior psychology and service scope into urban transportation planning.
Why couldn’t it be done before: Large-scale GIS spatial analysis was costly to calculate before the 2010s. The widespread use of mobile Internet and bus IC card data made it possible to calibrate behavior parameters, turning accessibility models from theory into practical applications.
Impact: It provided a methodological tool for quantitative evaluation in urban public transportation planning and has certain reference value in the field of domestic urban transportation planning.
Research Areas Thermal acoustics systems and cryogenic engineering (oscillating flow regenerators, traveling-wave thermoacoustic engines, heat pumps) Urban transportation planning and intelligent transportation systems (GIS accessibility, OD data, network reliability) Public health and epidemiology (schistosomiasis, thyroid function, diabetes prevention) Ecology and resource geology (bamboo invasion, shale gas resource assessment) Topological quantum field theory (topological order, Levin-Wen model) — possibly an independent researcher in recent years Methodological Evolution Since this profile is a mixture of multiple researchers, it is not possible to describe the methodological evolution of a single researcher. Looking at each research line individually: The thermal acoustics research line (2007→2014→2015) shows an engineering path from basic heat transfer experiments to system integration experiments and then to linear compressor drive; the transportation research line (2002→2007→2016→2017) gradually transitioned from heuristic methods using GIS + genetic algorithms to quantitative evaluations based on behavior psychology models. The papers on topological field theory that appeared in 2023-2025 (quantum double model, Levin-Wen model,瞬子在Young graph) represent a theoretical physicist from a completely different field, with no connection to any other papers.
Domain Impact This profile does not represent the domain influence of a single researcher. As a mixed profile, its total citations of 375 are spread across at least 5-6 completely different disciplines. None of these disciplines has reached a significant influence threshold (h-index=10). To evaluate the impact of any real researcher in this profile, it is necessary to perform manual de-duplication using institutional information, co-author networks, and email domain names. Recommendation: Any academic evaluation of this profile must first undergo manual de-duplication. Using this aggregated data will lead to serious misjudgments.

content:
Yingying Huang — Researcher Analysis Report Analysis Mode: fast | Analysis Time: 2026-03-16T00:06:23
Rating: Active Researcher (33.5/100) Basic Metrics Metric Value Institution Unknown h-index 6 Total Citations 86 Citations in Last 5 Years 0 Number of Papers Published 6 Papers Published in Top Journals 1 Publication Period 2016 - 2020 Semantic Scholar 2145496035 Research Trajectory From the paper list, it seems that “Yingying Huang” is likely a data merger of multiple researchers with the same name, rather than a single researcher’s academic trajectory. The papers cover various fields such as chemical biology (DNA-small molecule interactions, natural products), plasma physics (GS reconstruction), condensed matter physics (VO₂ phase transitions), surface physics, machine learning/BCI, nano-sensors, autonomous driving, and geophysical forward modeling. These fields have little overlap in methodology and community. It is rare for a single researcher to cover so many primary disciplines within such a short career period.
If we try to analyze it as a single-person trajectory: In the early years (2016–2017), her research focused on medicinal chemistry, using spectroscopy and molecular docking methods to study the interactions between naphthalimide-polyamine conjugates and DNA. She published 3 related papers, with a total of 50 citations, which contributed significantly to her h-index. In 2020, she shifted to natural product chemistry, isolating new active compounds (PPAPs) from endophytic fungi and plants like Hypericum roxburghii, and published research on COX-2 inhibitors. Starting in 2023, her research directions shifted towards EEG representation, piezoelectric sensors, BCI mapping, LLM-assisted driving, and neural operator geophysical modeling. This represents a complete shift from her earlier work.
The most reasonable explanation is that databases like Semantic Scholar have combined the papers of multiple authors with the same name, resulting in inconsistencies between the h-index (6), total citations (86), and publication period (2016–2020) compared to the actual paper list (ending in 2025). Additionally, the fact that “citations in last 5 years: 0” contradicts the existence of active new papers in 2025.
Breakthrough Works 1. Spectroscopic and molecular modeling methods to study the interaction between naphthalimide-polyamine conjugates and DNA (2016) Description: This paper systematically studied the interactions between naphthalimide-polyamine conjugates (compounds 1–7) and herring sperm DNA under physiological pH conditions. Using UV/FLUOROSPECTroscopy and molecular docking, it was found that the structure of the polyamine side chains influenced the binding mode.
Why couldn’t this be done before: There was a lack of systematic work combining spectroscopic data with in silico molecular docking methods. It was difficult to quantify the contribution of side chain length to DNA insertion/groove binding modes from both experimental and theoretical perspectives.
Impact: This paper received 34 citations, making it the researcher’s most cited paper. It provided an experimental-computational analysis framework for optimizing the structure of naphthalimide derivatives as potential anticancer drugs. This work also promoted the design of polyamine-insertion conjugates in subsequent research.
2. New bioactive secondary metabolites from the Anoectochilus roxburghii endophytic fungus Aspergillus versicolor (2020) Description: Six new compounds were isolated and identified from the endophytic fungus Aspergillus versicolor, including two new isobenzopyran lactones (versicoisochromanes A/B), two benzyl lactones, and a novel sterol. Their absolute configurations were determined using NMR and ECD.
Why couldn’t this be done before: Research on secondary metabolites from endophytic fungi like Hypericum roxburghii was rare before. The unique chemical environment of these fungi led to the discovery of rare structures. High-resolution mass spectrometry and ECD calculations made it easier to identify new compounds.
Impact: This paper received 16 citations, expanding the known chemical diversity of the Aspergillus genus. The discovered new structures provided a new framework for natural product research.
3. Hypersonins A-D, Polycyclic Polyprenylated Acylphloroglucinols with a 1,2-seco-Homoadamantane Architecture from Hypericum wilsonii (2020) Description: Four new polycyclic polyprenylated acylphloroglucinols with a 1,2-seco-adamantane structure were isolated from Hypericum wilsonii. These structures were confirmed using spectroscopy, X-ray crystallography, and ECD calculations.
Why couldn’t this be done before: The identification of PPAPs relied on high-field NMR and computational methods to determine their absolute configurations. These techniques have only become more common in recent years. The chemical taxonomy of this genus has also revealed the existence of such rare structures.
Impact: This paper received 13 citations, providing a new type of structure for PPAPs with a bicyclo[4.3.1]decane-3-methoxycarbonyl structure. This contributed to the study of structural diversity in this family.
4. A Knowledge-Driven Cross-view Contrastive Learning for EEG Representation (2023) Description: This paper proposed a cross-view contrastive learning framework that combined neural physiological knowledge to perform unsupervised/self-supervised representation learning on EEG signals. This approach helped solve the problem of scarcity of labeled EEG data.
Why couldn’t this be done before: The challenge in EEG contrastive learning is how to create meaningful positive/negative samples. Previous methods lacked a mechanism for integrating neuroscience knowledge into the contrastive learning framework. However, the rise of large-scale pre-training paradigms has made unsupervised EEG representation research feasible.
Impact: This work represents a significant shift in research direction. It combines neuroscience knowledge with modern self-supervised learning techniques, providing new ideas for BCI applications where data efficiency is limited.
Research Directions Medicinal chemistry and DNA interactions (naphthalimides/anthracene-polyamine conjugates, spectroscopy + molecular docking) Natural product chemistry (isolation, identification, and bioactivity evaluation of endophytic fungi and Hypericum roxburghii secondary metabolites) Brain-computer interfaces and EEG signal processing (contrastive learning, visual BCI, drawing assistance) Materials and sensors (VO₂ phase transitions, nano-piezoelectric sensors, contact angle physics) AI-assisted scientific computing (geophysical forward modeling, LLM-assisted driving perception) Methodology Evolution If we consider it as a single-person trajectory: In the early years (2016–2017), her methodology focused on experimental spectroscopy (UV/vis, fluorescence) combined with molecular docking. This was part of the traditional medicinal chemistry paradigm. In 2020, she shifted to standard natural product chemistry methods, including chromatography separation, NMR structure analysis, ECD absolute configuration determination, and in vitro activity evaluation. After 2023, her work shifted towards data-driven deep learning methods (contrastive learning, neural operators, large models), with little continuity between the previous two phases.
This methodological shift further supports the hypothesis that “Yingying Huang” is actually multiple researchers with the same name. A real single researcher would usually maintain some consistency in methodology or domain knowledge. However, this shift seems to be a sudden change.
Domain Impact Since this researcher’s profile is likely a merger of data from multiple researchers with the same name, it’s difficult to accurately assess the impact of “a single researcher” on the field. From the verified early papers, she made contributions to the study of the structure-activity relationship of naphthalimide-based DNA insertional agents (with 34 citations), and she contributed significantly to natural product chemistry by discovering new structures in Hypericum roxburghii. Overall, with an h-index of 6 and total citations of 86, she is considered a regular researcher in this field. However, it’s not yet possible to assess her impact on establishing new knowledge in the field. It’s recommended that when evaluating or reviewing papers, it’s necessary to confirm which “Yingying Huang” corresponds to which papers using ORCID or institutional websites before making an accurate academic assessment.
