content:
Intuition In this question, we find that if we flip a coin twice, then it is the same as flipping it zero times. Moreover, the only three ways to flip a coin is: Flip this coin Flip the coin before Flip the coin before this coin This means that if we pass this coin, we can no longer flip this coin. Approach In this case, we can iterate from front to end and flip every coin that is 0, and check whether the whole array is 1 at the end. Why is this method correct then? First we know that we cannot flip a coin after we pass this coin.
This means that we must flip this coin. If we do not flip this coin, then this coin will remain 0, which does not satisfy the quetion. Therefore, this step is required, missing this step will not give us the array we need.
We can also prove that this will lead us to the result for every array that can achieve this step. Because you have to flip this coin no matter what operation you did.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(N)$, N is the length of the array. Space complexity: $O(1)$. Code class Solution: def minOperations(self, nums: List[int]) -> int: cnt = 0 for i in range(len(nums) - 2): if nums[i] == 0: cnt += 1 nums[i] = 1 nums[i + 1] = 1 - nums[i + 1] nums[i + 2] = 1 - nums[i + 2] if nums[-1] == 1 and nums[-2] == 1: return cnt return -1
summary: 1. Minimum Operations to Make Binary Array Elements Equal to One I

content:
Today’s Problem https://leetcode.com/problems/find-the-punishment-number-of-an-integer/description/
Solution Approach Note that for numbers less than $1000^2$, the maximum number of partitioning schemes is $2^5 = 32$. This means that if we use DFS to determine whether to split a number at a certain position, each number can be checked in at most 32 scenarios.
Since $N \leq 1000$, we can solve this problem using brute-force methods.
Solution Method Use DFS to determine whether a number can be considered a “penalty number”.
Complexity Analysis Time complexity: $O(N\times 2^{log(N)})$, where N has the same meaning as described in the problem statement. Space complexity: $O(1)$. Code class Solution: def punishmentNumber(self, n: int) -> int: def check(x, now, s, nows, cnt): if now == 0: return (s + nows) == x if s > x: return False flag = check(x, now // 10, s, nows + now % 10 * (10 ** cnt), cnt + 1) if flag: return True flag |= check(x, now // 10, s + nows, now % 10, 1) return flag ans = 0 for i in range(n + 1): if check(i, i*i, 0, 0, 0): ans += i * i return ans class Solution: def punishmentNumber(self, n: int) -> int: punishmentNumbers = [0, 1, 9, 10, 36, 45, 55, 82, 91, 99, 100, 235, 297, 369, 370, 379, 414, 657, 675, 703, 756, 792, 909, 918, 945, 964, 990, 991, 999, 1000] ans = 0 for x in punishmentNumbers: if x > n: return ans ans += x * x return ans Results of the ordinary solution: Results of the fastest solution:

content:
Today’s Problem https://leetcode.com/problems/minimum-operations-to-exceed-threshold-value-ii/description
Solution Approach Use a heap to simulate the operations required by the problem.
Solution Steps Use the heap to extract the two smallest numbers from the list. Place $2 \times \min(x, y) + \max(x, y)$ back into the heap. If all extracted values are greater than or equal to k, then the operation is completed. Complexity Analysis Time complexity: $O(N \times \log(N))$, where N is the length of the sequence. Space complexity: $O(1)$, as the heap is constructed in-place using heapify, without any additional storage space needed. Code class Solution: def minOperations(self, nums: List[int], k: int) -> int: ans = 0 heapify(nums) x = heappop(nums) while(len(nums) > 0 and x < k): y = heappop(nums) nx = min(x, y) * 2 + max(x, y) heappush(nums, nx) x = heappop(nums) ans += 1 return ans
summary: 1. The minimum number of operations that exceed the threshold II

Idea This problem requires removing all occurrences of the substring “part” from the string “s”. Therefore, we can continuously check whether “s” contains “part”, and if it does, remove it. This process continues until “part” no longer appears in “s”.
Methods Method 1: Use a stack to achieve this. Traverse the string and push characters onto the stack in order. When several characters at the top of the stack completely match “part”, pop those characters from the stack.

Complexity Time complexity: $O(N\times M)$, where N is the length of s, and M is the length of “part”. Space complexity: $O(1)$ Code class Solution: def removeOccurrences(self, s: str, part: str) -> str: st = [] N = len(part) for ch in s: st.append(ch) if len(st) >= N: flag = True for i in range(1, N + 1): if st[-i] != part[-i]: print(st[-i], part[-i]) flag = False break if flag: for i in range(N): st.pop() return ''.join(st) Real Python class Solution: def removeOccurrences(self, s: str, part: str) -> str: while part in s: s = s.replace(part,"",1) return s

content:
Today’s Problem https://leetcode.com/problems/count-number-of-bad-pairs/description/
Solution Approach The key to solving this problem is to identify pairs of elements where the difference in indices is equal to the difference in values.
If we subtract the index difference from the distance between two elements, any pair with an equal result is considered a “good pair”.
That is: $j - i = nums[j] - nums[i] \Longrightarrow nums[j] - j = nums[i] - i$.
This simplifies the problem to counting the number of pairs $(i, j)$ in the array such that the transformed values are the same (i.e., they are good pairs).
Finally, by subtracting the number of good pairs from the total number of pairs, we can determine the number of bad pairs.
Solution Steps Calculate the total number of pairs, which is $N \times (N - 1) / 2$, where N is the length of the array. Subtract the index i from each element nums[i]. Count the number of pairs whose transformed values are the same (i.e., good pairs). Subtract the number of good pairs from the total number of pairs to get the number of bad pairs. Complexity Analysis Time complexity: $O(N)$, where N is the length of the array. Space complexity: $O(N)$, where N is the length of the array. Code [[[HYMTPH_0]]! image.png
summary: 1. Count the number of bad pairs

content:
Today’s Problem https://leetcode.com/problems/tuple-with-same-product/description/
Idea The core of this problem is to find tuples with the same product. Suppose there are n pairs of numbers with the same product. Then, the answer is $(^2_n) \times 4$. This is because we can choose any two pairs from the array to form a tuple that satisfies the condition.
So, how do we prove that these two pairs must be different?
Since all elements in the original array are unique, there are no duplicate numbers in the array. Therefore, if $a \times b = c \times d$, and $a \ne c$, then it follows that $b \ne d$. In other words, the two pairs must be different.
Method Therefore, we simply need to iterate through the entire array and count the number of pairs with the same product.
Tips Among the four dictionary types: [[[HYMTPH_1]], [[[HYMTPH_2]], [[[HYMTPH_3]], and {}, dict has the fastest execution time.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(N ^ 2)$, where N is the length of the array. Space complexity: $O(N)$, as we need to store the entire array. Code class Solution: def tupleSameProduct(self, nums: List[int]) -> int: nums.sort() cnt = dict() N = len(nums) for i in range(N): for j in range(i + 1, N): tmp = nums[i] * nums[j] if tmp not in cnt: cnt[tmp] = 1 else: cnt[tmp] += 1 ans = 0 for v in cnt.values(): ans += 4 * (v) * (v - 1) return ans
summary: 1. Tuples with the same product

content:
Today’s Problem https://leetcode.com/problems/divide-nodes-into-the-maximum-number-of-groups/description/
Solution Approach The key to this problem lies in the condition $|y - x| = 1$. With each step, the parity of the nodes changes. Therefore, when we encounter a situation where the parity of two nodes is the same (e.g., a cycle with three nodes and three edges), we should return $-1$. Otherwise, we simply need to enumerate the starting points of the graph and find the optimal starting node.
Thus, the solution approach is as follows:
Use DFS to determine if there are any parity conflicts in the graph. Use BFS to find the optimal starting point. Question: Why use BFS to find the starting point? Because we need to add the nodes that are connected to the current node into the next group.
Algorithm Steps Use DFS to determine if there are any parity conflicts in the graph. Use BFS to find the optimal starting point. Complexity Analysis Time complexity: $O(N^2)$ Space complexity: $O(N^2)$ Code class Solution: def magnificentSets(self, n: int, edges: List[List[int]]) -> int: vis = [0] * (n + 1) bvis = [0] * (n + 1) e = [[] for _ in range(n + 1)] for x,y in edges: e[x].append(y) e[y].append(x) ans = 0 clock = 0 def bfs(x): nonlocal clock clock += 1 bvis[x] = clock q = deque([(x,1)]) res = 1 while(len(q) > 0): now, dis = q.popleft() res = max(res, dis) for to in e[now]: if bvis[to] == clock: continue bvis[to] = clock q.append((to, dis + 1)) return res cur = 0 def dfs(x): nonlocal cur cur = max(cur, bfs(x)) # print(cur) tmp = 0 for to in e[x]: if vis[to] == 0: if vis[x] == 0: print("Warning!", x) vis[to] = -vis[x] tmp += dfs(to) else: if vis[to] != -vis[x]: return -1 return tmp for i in range(1, n + 1): if vis[i] == 0: cur = 0 vis[i] = 1 if dfs(i) < 0: return -1 # print("out: ", cur) ans += cur # print(bfs(5)) return ans
summary: 1. Divide the nodes into as many groups as possible.

content:
Today’s Problem https://leetcode.com/problems/making-a-large-island/description/
Idea This problem requires converting a 0 to a 1, in order to find the largest connected region. If we can calculate the size of each connected block of 0s in all four directions beforehand, then we can simply add up these sizes to get the answer.
Method First, calculate the size of each connected block and assign a label to each one, so that we don’t accumulate the same connected block multiple times.
Then, iterate through all the 0s and calculate the sum of their connected block sizes in all four directions after flipping them.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(N^2)$ Space complexity: $O(N^2)$ Code class Solution: def largestIsland(self, grid: List[List[int]]) -> int: islandCount = [0,0] dx = [0,0,1,-1] dy = [1,-1,0,0] n = len(grid) m = len(grid[0]) def dfs(x, y, cnt): grid[x][y] = cnt islandCount[cnt] += 1 for i in range(4): nx = x + dx[i] ny = y + dy[i] if nx < 0 or ny < 0 or nx >= n or ny >= m or grid[nx][ny] != 1: continue dfs(nx, ny, cnt) cnt = 1 for i in range(len(grid)): for j in range(len(grid[0])): if grid[i][j] == 1: cnt += 1 islandCount.append(0) dfs(i, j, cnt) ans = max(islandCount) for i in range(len(grid)): for j in range(len(grid[0])): if grid[i][j] == 0: tmp = 1 vis = set([]) for k in range(4): nx = i + dx[k] ny = j + dy[k] if nx < 0 or ny < 0 or nx >= n or ny >= m or grid[nx][ny] in vis: continue tmp += islandCount[grid[nx][ny]] vis.add(grid[nx][ny]) ans = max(ans, tmp) return ans
summary: Manufacturing the largest island possible
title: LeetCode Daily 2025-01-31

content:
Today’s problem https://leetcode.com/problems/redundant-connection/description/
Intuition Note that in a ring, every node have more than one index. Therefore, if we delete all the index that has one index, the remaining points will be a ring.
Approach Note that the label starts from 1 and ends at n, you would like to decrease index by one.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(N)$
Space complexity: $O(N)$
Code class Solution: def findRedundantConnection(self, edges: List[List[int]]) -> List[int]: N = len(edges) du = [0] * (N) E = [[] for _ in range(N)] for x,y in edges: x -= 1 y -= 1 du[x] += 1 du[y] += 1 E[x].append(y) E[y].append(x) q = deque([]) for i in range(N): if du[i] == 1: q.append(i) # print(du) while(len(q) > 0): x = q.popleft() du[x] = 0 for to in E[x]: if du[to] > 0: du[to] -= 1 if du[to] == 1: q.append(to) # print(du) loop = set([]) for i in range(N): if du[i] > 0: loop.add(i) for i in range(N - 1, -1, -1): x,y = edges[i] if x - 1 in loop and y - 1 in loop: return [x, y] return None
summary: 1. Redundant Connection
title: LeetCode Daily 2025-01-29

content:
Today’s Problem https://leetcode.com/problems/maximum-number-of-fish-in-a-grid/description/
Idea Find the size of the connected blocks.
Method Traverse the grid, accumulate the number of fish in each connected block, and finally return the maximum number of fish in the largest connected block.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(N\times M)$, with each cell being visited exactly once. Space complexity: $O(1)$, assuming no additional space is required for storing the original grid. Code class Solution: def findMaxFish(self, grid: List[List[int]]) -> int: ans = 0 dx = [0,0,1,-1] dy = [1,-1,0,0] for i in range(len(grid)): for j in range(len(grid[0])): if grid[i][j] == 0: continue tmp = grid[i][j] grid[i][j] = 0 q = deque([(i,j)]) while len(q) > 0: x,y = q.popleft() for k in range(4): nx = x + dx[k] ny = y + dy[k] if nx < 0 or ny < 0 or nx >= len(grid) or ny >= len(grid[0]) or grid[nx][ny] == 0: continue tmp += grid[nx][ny] grid[nx][ny] = 0 # print(i,j,nx, ny, tmp) q.append((nx, ny)) ans = max(ans, tmp) print(grid) return ans
summary: 1. The maximum number of fish that can be in a grid

content:
Today’s Problem https://leetcode.com/problems/course-schedule-iv/description/
Idea This problem requires determining whether one node is a ancestor of another node. To achieve this, we can maintain a set for each node, storing all its ancestor nodes. This set is then passed down to all child nodes.
Methods Use Depth-First Search to traverse all nodes. Create a set for each node and pass it to the child nodes. Complexity Time complexity: $O(N^3 + Q)$. Each edge is visited at most once, and the set passing operation is $O(N)$. Therefore, the total time complexity is $O(N^3 + Q)$. Space complexity: $O(N^2) + Q$. We need to store a set for each node, as well as store the answer. Code class Solution: def checkIfPrerequisite(self, numCourses: int, prerequisites: List[List[int]], queries: List[List[int]]) -> List[bool]: edges = [[] for _ in range(numCourses)] prereq = [set([_]) for _ in range(numCourses)] for x, y in prerequisites: edges[y].append(x) def dfs(x): for to in edges[x]: if len(prereq[to]) == 1: dfs(to) prereq[x] = prereq[x] | prereq[to] for i in range(numCourses): dfs(i) ans = [] for x, y in queries: if x in prereq[y]: ans.append(True) else: ans.append(False) return ans
summary: 1. Schedule IV
title: LeetCode Daily 2025-01-27

content:
Today’s Problem https://leetcode.com/problems/maximum-employees-to-be-invited-to-a-meeting/description/
Solution Approach This problem presents a directed graph with cycles. To identify these cycles, we can use topological sorting: find nodes with in-degree 0 and remove them along with their edges.
After this processing, all remaining nodes are within cycles.
In this problem, there are two ways for everyone to be seated:
A complete cycle, where each person’s left side neighbor is their favorite person. When two people are each other’s favorite, they form a complete cycle, and those who like them can form a chain pointing towards them. This structure is special because everyone can find their favorite person without forming a complete cycle. Therefore, multiple such structures can exist in the meeting room. Algorithm Steps Use topological sorting to find all cycles in the graph. Identify all special cases where two people are each other’s favorite. Return the size of the largest single cycle, or the total size of all special structures, whichever is larger. Complexity Analysis Time complexity: $O(N)$ Space complexity: $O(N)$ Code class Solution: def maximumInvitations(self, favorite: List[int]) -> int: N = len(favorite) du = [0] * N l = [1] * N for x in favorite: du[x] += 1 q = deque([]) for i in range(N): if du[i] == 0: q.append((i, 1)) while(len(q) > 0): x, leng = q.popleft() to = favorite[x] du[to] -= 1 l[to] = max(l[to], leng + 1) if du[to] == 0: q.append((to, leng + 1)) vis = [0] * N def dfs(i): to = favorite[i] vis[i] = 2 if vis[to] == 2: return 1 return dfs(to) + 1 ans = 0 res = 0 for i in range(N): if du[i] != 0 and vis[i] == 0: tmp = dfs(i) # print(i, tmp) if tmp == 2: # print(i, favorite[i], l[i], l[favorite[i]]) res += l[i] + l[favorite[i]] else: ans = max(ans, tmp) return max(ans, res)
summary: 1. The maximum number of employees who can attend the meeting is 2127.
title: LeetCode Daily 2025-01-26

content:
Today’s problem https://leetcode.com/problems/make-lexicographically-smallest-array-by-swapping-elements/description/
Intuition In this question, we are doing a sorting process that only difference less than limit can swap. Therefore, there forms “groups”. In a group, two sequential number has a difference less than limit. In this case, if we sort the numbers in a group, then we are all done. Approach We need to get the groups. We need to sort the array first. Then if the difference between two numbers are bigger than limit, then it would belong to two different groups. Then we sort the result for each group. Complexity Time complexity: $O(N\times log(N))$, N is the length of the array.
Space complexity: $O(N)$
Code class Solution: def lexicographicallySmallestArray(self, nums: List[int], limit: int) -> List[int]: sorted_nums = [] for idx, x in enumerate(nums): sorted_nums.append((x, idx)) sorted_nums.sort() # First we sort the array groups = [] tmp = [] for i in range(len(nums)): if i > 0 and sorted_nums[i][0] - sorted_nums[i-1][0] > limit: tmp.sort() groups.append(tmp) tmp = [] tmp.append(sorted_nums[i][1]) tmp.sort() groups.append(tmp) # Then we form groups idx = 0 pos = 0 ans = [0] * len(nums) for i in range(len(nums)): if pos == len(groups[idx]): pos = 0 idx += 1 ans[groups[idx][pos]] = sorted_nums[i][0] pos += 1 # Then we sort the groups return ans
summary: 1. Make Lexicographically Smallest Array by Swapping Elements
title: LeetCode Daily 2025-01-25

content:
Today’s Problem https://leetcode.com/problems/find-eventual-safe-states/description/
Note An outgoing node refers to any edge of that node, including self-edges.
Idea The only terminating node is a node with no outgoing edges.
Then, we need to identify which nodes are connected to these terminating nodes.
Therefore, we can use recursion (or any LIFO algorithm) to solve this problem.
Methods Use DFS. If a node is found that has no outgoing edges, it is a terminating node. If a self-edge is found, all nodes in the cycle are unsafe. If a node is only connected to safe nodes, then it itself is also safe. Complexity Time complexity: $O(N)$, as each node is visited only once. Space complexity: $O(N)$. Code class Solution: def eventualSafeNodes(self, graph: List[List[int]]) -> List[int]: n = len(graph) safety = [-1] * n vis = [0] * n ans = [] def dfs(x): if safety[x] != -1: return safety[x] if vis[x] == 1: safety[x] = 0 return 0 vis[x] = 1 if len(graph[x]) == 0: safety[x] = 1 return 1 res = 0 for to in graph[x]: res += dfs(to) if res == len(graph[x]): safety[x] = 1 else: safety[x] = 0 return safety[x] for i in range(n): if(dfs(i) == 1): ans.append(i) return ans
summary: 1. Find the final security status.

content:
Today’s problem https://leetcode.com/problems/count-servers-that-communicate/
Intuition Do what the question ask.
Approach First count the number of computers in each row and each column. Then count whether a computer has another computer that has the same row or column with it.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(NM)$, N, M, are the length and the width of the grid.
Space complexity: $O(NM)$
Code class Solution: def countServers(self, grid: List[List[int]]) -> int: cntR = [0] * len(grid) cntC = [0] * len(grid[0]) for i in range(len(grid)): for j in range(len(grid[0])): if grid[i][j]: cntR[i] += 1 cntC[j] += 1 ans = 0 for i in range(len(grid)): for j in range(len(grid[0])): if grid[i][j] and (cntR[i] > 1 or cntC[j] > 1): ans += 1 return ans
summary: 1. Count Servers that Communicate

content:
Today’s Problem https://leetcode.com/problems/map-of-highest-peak/description/
Idea Since the maximum absolute height difference between adjacent cells is 1, and the height of water cells is 0. Therefore, the answer is the Manhattan distance from each cell to the nearest water cell.
Method Use BFS to find the Manhattan distance from each cell to the nearest water cell.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(NM)$, where N and M are the number of rows and columns of the grid respectively. Space complexity: $O(NM)$ Code class Solution: def highestPeak(self, isWater: List[List[int]]) -> List[List[int]]: q = deque() ans = [[2005 for _ in range(len(isWater[0]))] for _ in range(len(isWater))] for i in range(len(isWater)): for j in range(len(isWater[0])): if isWater[i][j] == 1: q.append((i,j)) ans[i][j] = 0 dx = [0,0,1,-1] dy = [1,-1,0,0] while q: x,y = q.popleft() for i in range(4): nx = x + dx[i] ny = y + dy[i] if nx < 0 or ny < 0 or nx >= len(isWater) or ny >= len(isWater[0]) or ans[nx][ny] <= ans[x][y] + 1: continue ans[nx][ny] = ans[x][y] + 1 q.append((nx, ny)) return ans
summary: 1. The highest peak on the map

content:
Today’s Problem https://leetcode.com/problems/grid-game/description/
Solution Approach This is a special case where the grid has only two rows. Additionally, we need to consider the properties of all numbers in the grid.
Since robots cannot turn back once they move downward, this means that each robot only has one chance to switch to the second row.
What happens if Robot One switches to the second row at index $k$?
At this point, the non-zero numbers are divided into two parts: the numbers in the second row with indices less than $k$, and the numbers in the first row with indices greater than $k$.
Therefore, Robot Two can only choose one of these numbers to maximize its score. It can either take the remaining numbers from the first row or the remaining numbers from the second row. This is because once it switches to the second row, it cannot return to the first row.
Solution We need to calculate the sum of all numbers after index $k$ in the first row, and the sum of all numbers before index $k$ in the second row.
Techniques We use a prefix sum technique to solve this problem:
The sum of numbers after index $k$ in the first row can be calculated by subtracting $grid[k][0]$ from the previous sum (after index $k-1$). The sum of numbers before index $k$ in the second row can be calculated by adding $grid[k][1]$ to the previous sum. The cumulative sum required for the first row decreases over time, while the cumulative sum required for the second row increases. Therefore, when $max(sum1, sum2) > presentAns$, we can directly exit the loop. At this point, $sum2 > sum1$ and will continue to increase, so the answer won’t decrease anymore. (Here, $sum1$ is the current candidate sum for the first row, $sum2$ is the current candidate sum for the second row, and $presentAns$ is the current optimal answer when iterating to index $k$.)
Complexity Time complexity: $O(N)$, where $N$ is the number of columns in the grid. Space complexity: $O(1)$, as we only use a constant number of variables. Code class Solution: def gridGame(self, grid: List[List[int]]) -> int: x,y = sum(grid[0][1:]), 0 ans = x for i in range(1, len(grid[0])): x -= grid[0][i] y += grid[1][i - 1] if ans >= max(x,y): ans = max(x,y) else: return ans return ans
summary: 1. Grid Game
title: LeetCode Daily 2025-01-21

content:
Today’s Problem https://leetcode.com/problems/trapping-rain-water-ii/description/
Idea In Yesterday’s Problem, we discussed when it’s appropriate to use graph theory to solve problems.
Now, we can apply this criterion to this problem: in this problem, water can flow from four directions, which means there is a kind of persistence in the flow.
Therefore, we need to use graph theory.
The amount of water that can be stored in each cell depends on the height difference between that cell and its lowest neighbor (similar to the barrel effect taught in psychology classes—the capacity of a barrel is determined by the shortest plank).
It should be noted that water walls can also be considered as walls, and their height should be taken into account.
To determine the height of a wall, we first need to create a wall. But where should the wall be placed?
The most natural idea is to use the outermost boundary of the graph as the initial wall (just like in Example 2, where the outer boundary is used to store water).
Then, we find the lowest point on the wall and expand it into a new wall. In other words, if an internal neighbor is higher than the current wall point, then that neighbor becomes part of the new wall; otherwise, that internal point can store water, and the water level will be the same as the current wall point, thus forming a water wall.
Since we always choose the lowest point on the wall, the height of the rest of the wall will not be lower than that point. This means that the height of the current wall represents the maximum amount of water that can be stored inside.
Method Here, we can use a priority queue to efficiently find the lowest point on the wall.
Then, we follow the above algorithm:
Create the initial wall
Loop:
Find the lowest point on the wall
Create a new solid wall or water wall
End the loop
Add up the additional heights of all water walls
Complexity Time complexity: $O(NM\times log(NM))$ Space complexity: $O(NM)$ Code class Solution: def trapRainWater(self, heightMap: List[List[int]]) -> int: if len(heightMap) < 3 or len(heightMap[0]) < 3: return 0 dx = [0,0,1,-1] dy = [1,-1,0,0] vis = [] n, m = len(heightMap), len(heightMap[0]) q = [] for i in range(n): heappush(q, (heightMap[i][0], i, 0)) heappush(q, (heightMap[i][-1], i, m - 1)) vis.append((i, 0)) vis.append((i, m-1)) for i in range(1, m - 1): heappush(q, (heightMap[0][i], 0, i)) heappush(q, (heightMap[-1][i], n - 1, i)) vis.append((0, i)) vis.append((n-1, i)) vis = set(vis) ans = 0 while len(q) > 0: h, x, y = heappop(q) for i in range(4): nx, ny = x + dx[i], y + dy[i] if nx < 0 or ny < 0 or nx >= n or ny >= m or (nx,ny) in vis: continue if heightMap[nx][ny] < h: ans += h - heightMap[nx][ny] heappush(q, (max(h, heightMap[nx][ny]), nx, ny)) vis.add((nx, ny)) return ans
summary: 1. Collecting Rainwater II
title: LeetCode Daily 2025-01-19

content:
Today’s Problem https://leetcode.com/problems/neighboring-bitwise-xor/description/
Solution Approach According to the conclusion from yesterday’s problem ([HYMTPH_2]), we know that $a \space XOR \space a = 0$. Therefore, the result of $\large{XOR}_{i=0}^{n} \small derived[i]$ must be 0, because all the offsets of $original$ cancel each other out. Here, $n$ represents the last index of the sequence. Next, we prove that when $\large{XOR}_{i=0}^{n} \small derived[i] = 0$, we can reconstruct the complete $original$ sequence. Let $original[0] = 0$, and $original[k] = original[k-1] \space XOR \space derived[k]$. Now, we need to prove that $original[n] \space XOR \space original[0] = derived[n]$, meaning $original[0] = original[n] \space XOR \space derived[n]$. According to the recurrence relation, $original[n] = \large{XOR}_{i=0}^{n-1} \small derived[i] \space XOR \space original[0]$. Since $\large{XOR}{i=1}^{n} \small derived[i] = 0$, we have $original[n] \space XOR \space derived[n] = original[0] \space XOR \space \large{XOR}{i=0}^{n} \small derived[i] = 0 = original[0]$. Thus, the $original$ sequence is valid. Techniques We only need to check if the XOR sum of the sequence is 0. Since this is a binary sequence, we can group all 0s and 1s together. The XOR operation on all elements results in a value whose parity depends on the number of 1s. If the number of 1s is odd, the result is 1; otherwise, it’s 0. Therefore, the simplest approach is to sum all elements of the sequence and then determine its parity. Method Sum all elements of the sequence, and determine whether the result is odd or even.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(N)$, where $N$ is the length of the sequence. Space complexity: $O(1)$, as no additional variables are required. Code class Solution: def doesValidArrayExist(self, derived: List[int]) -> bool: return (sum(derived) & 1) == 0
summary: 1. Neighborwise XOR operation
title: LeetCode Daily 2025-01-17

content:
Today’s problem https://leetcode.com/problems/bitwise-xor-of-all-pairings/description/
Intuition According to the question, the formular of the output would be: $$ ans = \large{XOR}{\small i = 1}^{\small n} \large{XOR}{\small j = 1}^{\small m} nums1[i]\space xor \space nums2[j] $$ where $\large{XOR}$ means the operation that xor from $i$ to $n$. Here, $n$ means the length of $nums1$, and $m$ means the length of $nums2$. According to the properties of xor, xor satisfies the law of commutation and the law of association (more information can be seen here), so we can change the formular to: $$ ans = \large{XOR}{\small i = 1}^{\small n} (nums1[i]^m) \space xor \space \large{XOR}{\small j = 1}^{\small m} (nums2[j]^n) $$ According to the property that $A\space xor A = 0$, we now know that the result would be: $$ ans = \large{XOR}{\small i = 1}^{\small n} (nums1[i]^{m % 2}) \space xor \space \large{XOR}{\small j = 1}^{\small m} (nums2[j]^{n % 2}) $$
Approach Iterate two arrays and apply the formular above.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(N + M)$, N is the length of nums1, M is the length of nums2.
Space complexity: $O(1)$, no more space is used.
Code class Solution: def xorAllNums(self, nums1: List[int], nums2: List[int]) -> int: ans = 0 if len(nums1) % 2 == 1: for x in nums2: ans ^= x if len(nums2) % 2 == 1: for x in nums1: ans ^= x return ans
summary: 1. Bitwise XOR of All Pairings
title: LeetCode Daily 2025-01-16

content:
Today’s Question https://leetcode.com/problems/minimize-xor/description/
XOR Operation The following diagram shows the truth table of $XOR$: From this table, we can see that: if A and B have the same number of 1s, then $A \space XOR \space B = 0$; otherwise, $A \space XOR \space B = 1$.
Solution Approach The first condition of the problem, “the same number of 1s”, means that the number of 1s in each bit of the numbers must be equal.
This is because a number can have an infinite number of 0s, while the number of 1s is limited.
Therefore, we need to count the number of 1s in number2.
According to the XOR truth table, for a certain bit to become 0 after XORing, that bit in the result must also be set to 1, thereby canceling out the 1 in the corresponding bit of number1.
To minimize the result, we should eliminate the 1s in number1 from the highest bits first.
If number2 has more 1s than number1, we need to add additional 1s to the result.
To minimize the result, these additional 1s should be added from the lowest bits to the highest bits.
Algorithm Steps Use the $bit_count()$ function to count the number of 1s in num2. Iterate from the highest bit to the lowest bit, eliminating the 1s in number1 first. Iterate from the lowest bit to the highest bit, adding the remaining 1s as needed. Complexity Analysis Time complexity: $O(log_2(N))$, where N is the size of the input numbers. Space complexity: $O(1)$, as only a few variables are used. Code class Solution: def minimizeXor(self, num1: int, num2: int) -> int: cnt,ans = num2.bit_count(), 0 for i in range(31, -1, -1): if cnt > 0 and (num1 & (1 << i)) > 0: ans += (1 << i) cnt -= 1 for i in range(31): if cnt > 0 and (ans & (1 << i)) == 0: ans += (1 << i) cnt -= 1 return ans
summary: 1. Minimizing XOR operations
title: LeetCode Daily 2025-01-15

content:
Today’s problem https://leetcode.com/problems/find-the-prefix-common-array-of-two-arrays/description/
Intuition In this question, the only three ways that the answer will increase one is:
$A[i] == B[i]$. $A[i]$ appears in $B$. $B[i]$ appears in $A$. Approach Iterate through the Array, then check for those three situation. Note that situation 1 conflicts with situation 2 & 3. That is, if cnt is add by 1 through situation 1, then situation 2 & 3 will not increase cnt. But situation 2 & 3 could increase cnt. Complexity Time complexity: $O(N)$, N is the length of the list.
Space complexity: $O(N)$, because we need to store a set.
Code class Solution: def findThePrefixCommonArray(self, A: List[int], B: List[int]) -> List[int]: ans = [] cntA = set([]) cntB = set([]) cnt = 0 for i in range(len(A)): if A[i] == B[i]: cnt += 1 else: if A[i] in cntB: cnt += 1 if B[i] in cntA: cnt += 1 cntA.add(A[i]) cntB.add(B[i]) ans.append(cnt) return ans
summary: 1. Find the Prefix Common Array of Two Arrays
title: LeetCode Daily 2025-01-14

content:
Today’s Problem https://leetcode.com/problems/minimum-length-of-string-after-operations/
Solution Approach In this problem, each character is independent of the others. Therefore, we can analyze each character individually. If a character appears an odd number of times, we will delete it starting from the left until only one character remains. If a character appears an even number of times, then two characters will remain in the end. Solution Method Therefore, we just need to count the number of times each character appears, and then determine its parity.
Complexity Analysis Time complexity: $O(N)$, where N is the length of the string. Space complexity: $O(N)$, as we use a counter to store the frequency of each character. Code class Solution: def minimumLength(self, s: str) -> int: cnt = Counter([ch for ch in s]) ans = 0 for x,v in cnt.items(): if v % 2 == 0: ans += 2 else: ans += 1 return ans
summary: 1. 操作后的字符串最短长度
title: LeetCode Daily 2025-01-13

content:
Today’s Problem https://leetcode.com/problems/check-if-a-parentheses-string-can-be-valid/
Solution Approach To construct a valid parentheses string, we need to find an ‘(’ for each ‘)’ on its left side, and an ‘)’ for each ‘(’ on its right side. When traversing from left to right, we can satisfy the first condition first.
When encountering a ‘)’, there are three ways to match it with an ‘(’: one is using an existing ‘:’, another is finding an unlocked ‘)’, and thirdly, if the ‘)’ itself is unlocked, it can be converted into an ‘:’.
Assuming we have found matches for all ‘)’, the next step is to find an ‘)’ for the remaining ‘:’s. The only way to do this is to find an unlocked bracket, as all ‘)’s have been matched.
Solution First, if the string length is odd, it is impossible to form a valid parentheses string. In this case, directly use return False.
Maintain two arrays: $anyBracket$ to store the indices of all unlocked brackets, and $openBracket$ to store the indices of all ‘:’s.
As we traverse the entire string, we will encounter the following situations:
If the current character is an unlocked bracket, push its index onto the $anyBracket$ stack. If the current character is ‘:’, push its index onto the $openBracket$ stack. If the current character is ‘)’, we need to find an ‘:’ to match it. First, pop an ‘:’ from the $openBracket$ stack, which helps us find an ‘)’ for the ‘:’. If the $openBracket$ stack is empty, take an unlocked bracket from the $anyBracket$ stack (to act as an ‘:’). If both stacks are empty, then return False, because no ‘:’ can be found for the current ‘)’. After traversing, there may still be unmatched ‘:’s in the $openBracket$ stack.
Now, iterate through each unmatched ‘:’ in the $openBracket$ stack, and check if there is a bracket in the $anyBracket$ stack with a larger index (i.e., an unlocked bracket on its right side).
If there is such a bracket, it means we have successfully found an ‘)’ for the ‘:)’, and the match is successful! Otherwise, it is not possible to match, and return False. If the number of elements remaining in the $anyBracket$ stack is even (since we already checked the parity earlier, this must be true), then return True, and we are done!
Important Techniques Why use stacks for both $openBracket$ and $anyBracket$? Because ‘)’ always tries to match with the closest ‘:’ on its left side. Therefore, we need a FIFO data structure to retrieve the “nearest element”. Complexity Analysis Time complexity: $O(N)$, where N is the length of the string.
Space complexity: $O(N)$, as we store the indices of all brackets.
Code [[[HYMTPH_0]]
summary: 1. Determine whether the parentheses string is valid or not.

content:
Today’s Problem https://leetcode.com/problems/construct-k-palindrome-strings/description/
Idea In a palindrome string, at most one character can have an odd number of occurrences. Therefore, to construct $k$ palindromes, the number of characters that have an odd number of occurrences can be at most $k$.
Additionally, since there are only 26 different characters, if $26 \leq k$, the result will definitely be true. However, if the length of $s$ is less than $k$, the result will be false.
Let’s prove this. Let’s denote the number of characters with an odd number of occurrences as $cntO$. The total number of occurrences for all other characters is $2 \times cntE$.
If all characters have an even number of occurrences, then as long as $k \leq N$ (where $N$ is the total length of $s$), it is possible to construct a palindrome. This is because we can place one character at the beginning of the palindrome and the corresponding repeated character at the end, thus maintaining the palindrome structure.
Since $k \leq N$, we have $k \leq cntO + 2 \times cntE$, which implies $k - cntO \leq 2 \times cntE$. This means that all characters with an odd number of occurrences can form a palindrome, totaling $cntO$ such palindromes.
We have already proven that when all characters have an even number of occurrences, as long as $k \leq N$, it is possible to construct a palindrome. Therefore, if $cntO \le k$, the result is true; otherwise, it is false.
In summary, the problem boils down to determining whether $k \leq N$ when all characters have an even number of occurrences. This condition is always true.
Tips Since there are only 26 different characters, if $26 \leq k$, the result will definitely be true.
Solution Method Just count the number of occurrences for each character, and then check if the number of characters with an odd number of occurrences does not exceed $k$.
Complexity Analysis Time complexity: $O(N)$, where $N$ is the length of the string. Space complexity: $O(1)$, as we only need to store the counts of 26 characters. Code class Solution: def canConstruct(self, s: str, k: int) -> bool: cnt = [0] * 26 if len(s) < k: return False if k > 25: return True # The code that makes the code run very fast. for ch in s: cnt[ord(ch) - ord('a')] += 1 x = 0 for i in range(26): if cnt[i] % 2 == 1: x += 1 return x <= k
summary: 1. Construct K palindrome strings.

content:
Today’s problem https://leetcode.com/problems/word-subsets/
Intuition The brute method would take $O(N^2\times (L+D))$ time, while $N$ is the length of the words, $L$ is the length of each word, and $D$ is the size of the dictionary (that is, 26 characters). However, $N \le 10^4$, which means that we cannot use brute method. Then we found out that we do not need to compare all $N$ words, instead, we only need to compare the maximum of the occurence of each characters in words2. For example, if ‘a’ appears 3 times in $words2[1]$, 2 times in $words2[2]$, 4 times in $words2[3]$, then ‘a’ must appears at least 4 times in a $words1[i]$ to add one to the answer. Therefore, all we need is to count the occurence of each character in word1, and count the maximun occurence of each character in every word2.
Approach Count the occurence of each character in word1, and count the maximun occurence of each character in every word2.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(N \times (L + D))$, while $N$ is the length of the words, $L$ is the length of each word, and $D$ is the size of the dictionary (that is, 26 characters).
Space complexity: $O(N \ times D)$.
Code class Solution: def wordSubsets(self, words1: List[str], words2: List[str]) -> List[str]: wordcnt1, wordcnt2 = [], [0] * 26 a = ord('a') for word in words1: cnt = [0] * 26 for ch in word: x = ord(ch) - a cnt[x] += 1 wordcnt1.append(cnt) for word in words2: cnt = [0] * 26 for ch in word: x = ord(ch) - a cnt[x] += 1 for j in range(26): wordcnt2[j] = max(wordcnt2[j], cnt[j]) ans = [] for i in range(len(words1)): flag = True for k in range(26): if wordcnt1[i][k] < wordcnt2[k]: flag = False break if flag: ans.append(words1[i]) return ans
summary: 1. Word Subsets

content:
Today’s Problem https://leetcode.com/problems/count-prefix-and-suffix-pairs-i/description/
Solution Just simulate according to the problem description!
Approach Use two loops to iterate through the string, checking the prefix and suffix of each character one by one.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(N^2\times L)$, where N is the length of the word array, and L is the length of the string. Space complexity: $O(1)$ Code class Solution: def countPrefixSuffixPairs(self, words: List[str]) -> int: def checkpre(str1, str2): if len(str1) > len(str2): return False return str1 == str2[:len(str1)] def checksuf(str1, str2): if len(str1) > len(str2): return False return str1 == str2[len(str2) - len(str1):] ans = 0 for i in range(len(words)): for j in range(i+1, len(words)): if checkpre(words[i],words[j]) and checksuf(words[i], words[j]): ans += 1 return ans class Solution: def countPrefixSuffixPairs(self, words: List[str]) -> int: ans = 0 for i in range(len(words)): for j in range(i+1, len(words)): if words[j].startswith(words[i]) and words[j].endswith(words[i]): ans += 1 return ans
summary: 1. 统计了前缀和后缀的组合数量 I

content:
Today’s Problem https://leetcode.com/problems/minimum-number-of-operations-to-move-all-balls-to-each-box/description
Solution Approach For each position, it’s easy to see that the answer at that position is equal to the sum of the distances from i to all 1s. That is: $ans[i]=\sum_{j=0}^{n-1} boxes[j] \times abs(j-i)$. Now, imagine a pointer moving from left to right (from 1 to n), calculating the result step by step. It can be observed that the difference between $ans[i]$ and $ans[i+1]$ is equal to the number of 1s between 1 and i minus the number of 1s between i+1 and n. That is: $ans[i+1] - ans[i] = \sum_{j=0}^{i} boxes[j] - \sum_{j=i+1}^{n-1} boxes[j]$. Therefore, by calculating the number of 1s on the left of i and the total number of 1s in the sequence, we can find all answers in $O(N)$ time.
Algorithm Steps First, use the formula $ans[0]=\sum_{j=1}^{n} boxes[j] \times j$ to calculate $ans[0]$ and the total number of 1s in the sequence. This requires iterating through the entire sequence. Next, for each i (from 1 to n-1), use the formula $ans[i+1] - ans[i] = \sum_{j=0}^{i} boxes[j] - \sum_{j=i+1}^{n-1} boxes[j]$ to compute the value recursively. Since $ans[i+1] - ans[i] = \sum_{j=1}^{i} boxes[j] - \sum_{j=i+1}^{n} boxes[j] = 2 \times \sum_{j=0}^{i} boxes[j] - \sum_{j=0}^{n-1} boxes[j]$, we can maintain a count of 1s on the left during the iteration, and then apply the above formula.
Complexity Analysis Time complexity: $O(N)$, where N is the length of boxes. Space complexity: $O(N)$, used for storing the answer array. Code class Solution: def minOperations(self, boxes: str) -> List[int]: now = 0 cnt1 = 0 for i in range(len(boxes)): if boxes[i] == '1': now += i cnt1 += 1 ans = [now] * len(boxes) now_cnt1 = 0 for i in range(1, len(boxes)): if boxes[i-1] == '1': now_cnt1 += 1 ans[i] = ans[i-1] + 2 * now_cnt1 - cnt1 return ans
summary: 1. The minimum number of operations required to move all balls to each box.

content:
Today’s Problem https://leetcode.com/problems/unique-length-3-palindromic-subsequences/description/
Idea Since the problem requires counting the number of different subsequences, the maximum count is $26*26 = 676$. Therefore, we only need to count how many different characters are between two occurrences of the same character.
Method First, find the first and last occurrence of each character. Then, traverse between l and r to count how many different characters there are.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(kN)$, where k is the number of different characters. Here, k is 26 different letters, and N is the length of the string. Space complexity: $O(N)$ Code class Solution: def countPalindromicSubsequence(self, s: str) -> int: st = [inf] * 26 en = [-1] * 26 a = ord('a') for i,ch in enumerate(s): nch = ord(ch) - a st[nch] = min(st[nch], i) en[nch] = max(en[nch], i) ans = 0 for x in range(26): if en[x] <= st[x]: continue # print(en[x], st[x], x) vis = set([]) for i in range(st[x] + 1, en[x]): vis.add(s[i]) ans += len(vis) return ans
summary: 1. Three different palindrome subsequences of length 3

content: class Solution: def waysToSplitArray(self, nums: List[int]) -> int: now,summ, ans = 0, 0, sum(nums) for num in nums[:-1]: now += num if now >= summ - now: ans += 1 return ans
summary: The number of solutions for splitting a set of elements.

content: Code class Solution: def vowelStrings(self, words: List[str], queries: List[List[int]]) -> List[int]: sumWords = [0] * (len(words) + 1) vowels = set(['a','e','i','o','u']) for idx, word in enumerate(words): sumWords[idx] = sumWords[idx-1] if word[0] in vowels and word[-1] in vowels: sumWords[idx] += 1 ans = [] for x,y in queries: ans.append(sumWords[y] - sumWords[x-1]) return ans
summary: 1. Counting the number of vowel characters within a range

content:
Today’s problem https://leetcode.com/problems/maximum-score-after-splitting-a-string/description
Intuition Do what the question asks.
Approach Do what the question asks. Iterate from the back to the front to count how many 1s, iterate from the front to the back to count how many 2s. To speed up the process, we can first count how many 1s are there in the whole sequence, then use another iteration to count the remaining 1s in the sequence by doing a subtraction. Same to the question in Jan.03.2025, the number of 1s in index i + 1 to n = the number of 1s in the sequence - the number of 1s in index 1 to i.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(N)$, N is the length of s.
Space complexity: $O(1)$, only a few new variables are stored.
Code class Solution: def maxScore(self, s: str) -> int: num1 = 0 for ch in s: if ch == '1': num1 += 1 now0, now1, ans = 0, 0, 0 for ch in s[:-1]: if ch == '0': now0 += 1 else: now1 += 1 ans = max(ans, now0 + num1 - now1) return ans
summary: 1. Maximum Score After Splitting a String
